<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Machine Drawing — Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Drawing** is the standardized graphical language of communication used by engineers to convey the geometry, dimensions, tolerances, and manufacturing details of machine components and assemblies. It conforms to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** code of practice, primarily governed by **IS 696 : 1972** (general engineering drawings), **IS 2709 : 1981** (dimensioning), and **IS 9190 : 1979** (limits and fits). Machine drawings act as the **legal and technical contract** between the designer, the manufacturer, and the quality inspector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A machine drawing is NOT just a picture. It is a *precise engineering document* that must unambiguously specify every feature, dimension, tolerance, surface finish, and material so that a third party can manufacture the part without ever seeing the original component.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engineering Blueprint is a Universal Language"

Imagine you are sending a recipe to a friend in another country who has never tasted the dish. You cannot send a photo alone — you must write a **list of ingredients** (materials), **exact quantities** (dimensions), **cooking temperatures** (tolerances), and **step-by-step procedure** (assembly sequence). A machine drawing is precisely this kind of *foolproof recipe* for a mechanical part. The lines, symbols, and conventions are the "grammar" of this universal engineering language, ensuring that a designer in Kerala and a machinist in Ludhiana interpret the drawing identically.

---

## 1.3 BIS Code of Practice — The Backbone of Indian Engineering Drawings

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** issues standardized codes that govern every aspect of engineering drawing in India. The most relevant codes for KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| BIS Code | Subject / Domain |
|---|---|
| **IS 696 : 1972** | General engineering drawing practice (lines, projections, sections) |
| **IS 2709 : 1981** | Dimensioning of technical drawings |
| **IS 9190 : 1979** | Limits and fits for engineering |
| **IS 8000** | Geometrical product specifications (GPS) and GD&T base |
| **IS 11669** | Surface texture symbols |
| **IS 10714** | Engineering drawing — simplified representation |

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** is the national standards body of India, formerly known as the **ISI (Indian Standards Institution)**. All KTU exam questions on machine drawing conventions must follow these IS standards unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 1.4 Types of Lines — The Visual Vocabulary

Every engineering drawing is composed of **eight principal types of lines** (as per IS 696). Each line type has a specific meaning and thickness ratio.

| Line Type | Appearance | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Visible/Object Line** | Continuous thick line | Visible edges and outlines of an object |
| **Hidden Line** | Short dashes of medium thickness | Hidden edges behind a surface |
| **Center Line** | Long dash, short dash, long dash | Axes of symmetry, centers of holes/circles |
| **Section Line** | Continuous thin line with arrows | Cutting plane indicators |
| **Hatching/Section Line** | Continuous thin lines at 45° | Cut surfaces in sectional views |
| **Construction Line** | Continuous thin line (faint) | Auxiliary construction in geometry |
| **Dimension Line** | Continuous thin line terminated by arrows | To show dimensions |
| **Leader/Extension Line** | Continuous thin line | To extend a feature to a dimension |
| **Break Line** | Continuous thick freehand or straight jagged | To indicate a part has been shortened |
| **Phantom Line** | Long-dash, two short dashes | Alternate positions, repeated detail |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thickness Rule (BIS):** Thick lines (visible, section cuts) are usually **0.5 mm to 0.7 mm** thick. Thin lines (dimensioning, hatching, construction) are roughly **half** that thickness, maintaining a constant ratio of **2:1** throughout the drawing.

---

## 1.5 Dimensioning — Quantifying Geometry

**Dimensioning** is the process of adding numerical values (lengths, angles, diameters) to a drawing to define the size and location of features. The four elements of a dimension are:
1. **Dimension line** (with arrowheads)
2. **Extension lines** (projecting from the feature)
3. **Numerals** (the value, usually in mm without units)
4. **Leader line** (for notes and diameters on circles)

### Fundamental Rules of Dimensioning (BIS SP 46 / IS 2709)

- Dimensions should be placed **outside the view** wherever possible.
- Dimensions should be read from the **bottom or right side** of the drawing.
- Avoid dimensioning to **hidden lines** unless absolutely necessary.
- **Never repeat** the same dimension in two different views.
- Use **symbols** for features: $\phi$ for diameter, $R$ for radius, $\square$ for square, $\angle$ for angle, $\pitchfork$ for counterbore, $\vee$ for countersink.
- Dimensions should be given in a way that manufacturing is most convenient.

---

## 1.6 Scales of Drawing

A **scale** is the ratio of the **drawing size** to the **actual size** of the object. Since engineering components range from microscopic gears to massive ship propellers, scaling is essential.

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length (L}_d\text{)}}{\text{Actual Length (L}_a\text{)}}$$

| Category | Reduction | Enlargement | Full Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recommended Scales (BIS)** | $1:2$, $1:5$, $1:10$, $1:20$, $1:50$, $1:100$, $1:200$ | $2:1$, $5:1$, $10:1$ | $1:1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In scale notation like **1:5**, the first figure is the *drawing size* and the second is the *actual object size*. So $1:5$ means 1 unit on paper represents 5 units in reality (reduction).

---

## 1.7 Sectional Views — Revealing the Interior

A **sectional view** (or section) is obtained by hypothetically cutting the object with a cutting plane and removing the portion between the observer and the cutting plane. This reveals the **internal features** (holes, slots, webs) that would otherwise be hidden.

**Types of Sections:**
- **Full Section** — Cutting plane passes completely through the object.
- **Half Section** — One half shows external view, other half shows section (for symmetrical parts).
- **Offset Section** — Cutting plane is bent to pass through multiple features.
- **Aligned Section** — Features are rotated to align with the cutting plane.
- **Removed Section** — Section is drawn outside the main view.
- **Revolved Section** — Section is rotated 90° and drawn on the view itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatching Convention (BIS):** Hatching lines are drawn at **45°** to the horizontal, spaced **2 mm to 3 mm** apart, and use **continuous thin lines**. Different parts in an assembly are hatched at *different spacings, directions, or offsets* to distinguish them.

---

## 1.8 Geometric Tolerances and GD&T — The Precision Language

**Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T)** is a symbolic language (defined by **ASME Y14.5** and **ISO 1101** / **IS 8000**) used to communicate engineering tolerances on a part's geometry. It goes beyond traditional plus/minus tolerancing by controlling **form, orientation, location, and run-out** of features.

### The 5 Groups of GD&T Symbols (14 total symbols)

| Group | Symbols |
|---|---|
| **Form** | Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity |
| **Orientation** | Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity |
| **Location** | Position, Concentricity/Concentricity, Symmetry |
| **Profile** | Profile of a Line, Profile of a Surface |
| **Run-out** | Circular Run-out, Total Run-out |

> [!NOTE]
> **Datum:** A datum is a theoretically exact reference (point, line, or plane) used to establish geometric tolerances. Datums are identified by a letter (A, B, C…) inside a square with a leader to the feature.

---

## 1.9 Simple 2D Sketches — The Starting Point of Design

A **2D sketch** is a flat, two-dimensional representation created in CAD software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, Fusion 360) using primitive entities: **lines, circles, arcs, polylines, splines, and points**. Sketches form the *foundation* of 3D modelling — every 3D feature (extrude, revolve, sweep, loft) begins from a 2D sketch.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab exams, a "Simple 2D Sketch" usually means drawing standard machine elements (a stepped shaft, a keyway, a bushing) in AutoCAD with proper BIS conventions, dimensions, and section views.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why BIS Conventions Are Non-Negotiable

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** standards exist to eliminate ambiguity. Before standardization:
- Two engineers could interpret the same drawing differently.
- Manufacturing errors were common.
- International trade was impossible.

By following IS 696, IS 2709, and IS 9190, every draughtsman in India produces a drawing that is **self-explanatory, reproducible, and legally defensible** in patent disputes or quality audits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 exam questions often test whether a student knows the *correct BIS convention* versus a "casual drawing practice". For example, a center line should always be drawn with **alternating long and short dashes**, never as a continuous line.

---

## 2.2 Analytical Breakdown of Line Types

### 2.2.1 Visible Object Line
- **Purpose:** Outlines edges that are directly visible to the observer.
- **Thickness:** Thick line ($0.5$ to $0.7$ mm typical).
- **Priority:** In any view, visible lines take **precedence** over hidden and center lines when they coincide.

### 2.2.2 Hidden Line
- **Purpose:** Represents edges hidden behind a surface.
- **Thickness:** Medium thin line.
- **Pattern:** Short dashes (~$2$ mm long) with gaps of ~$1$ mm.

### 2.2.3 Center Line
- **Purpose:** Marks axes of symmetry, axes of holes, and centers of circular features.
- **Thickness:** Thin line.
- **Pattern:** Long dash (~ $20$ mm) — short gap — short dash (~ $3$ mm) — short gap, repeated.
- **Extension:** Center lines extend slightly beyond the feature (~$3$ to $5$ mm).

### 2.2.4 Section Line (Cutting Plane Line)
- **Purpose:** Indicates the path of the cutting plane.
- **Thickness:** Thick line.
- **Arrows:** Show the viewing direction at each end.

### 2.2.5 Hatching Line
- **Purpose:** Indicates the cut surface in a section view.
- **Thickness:** Thin line.
- **Angle:** Always $45^{\circ}$ to the principal outline.

---

## 2.3 Dimensioning — The Four Element System Explained

A complete dimension consists of four logical components:

1. **Extension Lines (Witness Lines):**
   - Originate from the feature, extend beyond the dimension line.
   - Drawn as continuous thin lines.
   - Leave a small gap ($\approx 1.5$ mm) between the feature and the start of the extension line.

2. **Dimension Line:**
   - Continuous thin line, placed between extension lines.
   - Terminated with **filled triangular arrowheads** (45° apex) at both ends, OR **oblique strokes** (45° short lines) at the ends.
   - Numerical value is placed at the **center** of the dimension line, **above and broken**, OR placed continuously above the unbroken line.

3. **Numerical Value (Dimension Figure):**
   - Placed in mm (no units written).
   - For diameters, prefix with $\phi$ symbol.
   - For radii, prefix with $R$.
   - Size of numerals is standardized (typically $3$ mm to $5$ mm).

4. **Leader Line:**
   - A thin line from a note or dimension to the feature.
   - Ends with an arrowhead or a small dot (the dot inside the feature, arrow at the text).

### Method of Placing Dimensions

| Method | Usage |
|---|---|
| **Aligned System** | Dimension values placed parallel to the dimension line, reading from the bottom-right. |
| **Unidirectional System** | All numerals are placed horizontal, read from the bottom. (Preferred in modern CAD) |

---

## 2.4 Scales — The Mathematical Relationship

The fundamental scale equation is:

$$S = \frac{L_d}{L_a}$$

where $L_d$ is the length on the drawing and $L_a$ is the actual length of the object.

**If $S < 1$** → Reduction scale (object is too large to draw full size).
**If $S = 1$** → Full size (object is drawn at actual dimensions).
**If $S > 1$** → Enlargement scale (object is too small to see details).

### Reverse Calculation (Required for Exam)

To find the *drawing length* given the *actual length* and the *scale ratio*:

$$L_d = L_a \times S$$

To find the *actual length* given the *drawing length*:

$$L_a = \frac{L_d}{S}$$

### Worked Example 1: Reduction Scale
A component is $250$ mm long. Using scale $1:5$, find the length on the drawing.

$$L_d = L_a \times S = 250 \times \frac{1}{5} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

### Worked Example 2: Enlargement Scale
A thread profile of $4$ mm pitch is drawn at scale $10:1$. Find the drawing size.

$$L_d = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 2.5 Sectional Views — Engineering Logic

The **cutting plane concept** is a hypothetical tool — the part is *not* actually cut. The drafting convention shows the observer what the part *would* look like if a saw had sliced it open and the front half was removed.

### Hatching Rules
- Hatching is applied to the **solid material that has been cut**.
- Hatching is **NOT applied** to:
  - Shafts
  - Bolts, nuts, rivets
  - Keys, pins
  - Spokes of wheels, webs
  - Ribs (when the cutting plane is longitudinal)
- Hatching should be at **45°** to the principal outline of the section.
- Adjacent parts in an assembly are hatched in **opposite directions** or with **different spacing**.

### Section View Identification
- A section view is **labeled** with a capital letter (A-A, B-B) matching the cutting plane label.
- The cutting plane is drawn using a **thick line** with arrows showing viewing direction.
- The cutting plane is identified with **capital letters** at each end.

---

## 2.6 GD&T — The 14 Geometric Symbols and Their Meaning

| Symbol | Characteristic | Category | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| — | Straightness | Form | Element must be a straight line |
| ⌖ | Flatness | Form | Surface must lie between two parallel planes |
| ◯ | Circularity (Roundness) | Form | Circle must lie between two concentric circles |
| ⌭ | Cylindricity | Form | Cylinder must lie between two coaxial cylinders |
| ∥ | Parallelism | Orientation | Feature must be parallel to a datum |
| ⟂ | Perpendicularity | Orientation | Feature must be 90° to a datum |
| ∠ | Angularity | Orientation | Feature must be at a specified angle to a datum |
| ⊕ | Position (True Position) | Location | Feature must be located within a tolerance zone |
| ◎ | Concentricity / Coaxiality | Location | Axes of features must coincide |
| ⌯ | Symmetry | Location | Feature must be symmetric about a datum |
| ⌒ | Profile of a Line | Profile | Tolerance zone for a line element |
| ⌢ | Profile of a Surface | Profile | Tolerance zone for a surface |
| ↗ | Circular Run-out | Run-out | Variation during one revolution |
| ↗↗ | Total Run-out | Run-out | Variation across the entire surface |

### The Three Datums Reference Frame (DRF)

In GD&T, **datums** establish a coordinate system:
- **Datum A, B, C** (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary).
- A datum is identified by a **letter inside a square box**, connected to the datum feature via a **triangle** (filled for surface, blank for extension).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GD&T uses **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and **Least Material Condition (LMC)** modifiers. MMC = the condition where the part has the most material (e.g., largest shaft, smallest hole). LMC = the least material condition. These are commonly tested in KTU questions.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Symbol Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|
| $S = L_d / L_a$ | Scale ratio (drawing/actual) | Dimensionless |
| $L_d = L_a \times S$ | Drawing length from actual | mm |
| $L_a = L_d / S$ | Actual length from drawing | mm |
| $\phi$ | Diameter prefix | mm |
| $R$ | Radius prefix | mm |
| $\square$ | Square feature prefix | mm |
| $\pitchfork$ | Counterbore symbol | — |
| $\vee$ | Countersink symbol | — |
| $45^{\circ}$ | Standard hatching angle | degrees |
| $0.5$ to $0.7$ mm | Thick line thickness | mm |
| Half of thick | Thin line thickness | mm |
| MMC | Maximum Material Condition | Modifier |
| LMC | Least Material Condition | Modifier |
| RFS | Regardless of Feature Size | Modifier |
| DRF | Datum Reference Frame | Concept |
| 14 | Total GD&T symbols | Count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** In KTU answer scripts, never use the bare pipe symbol `\vert` in a sentence when you mean absolute value — but inside a table row, the markdown parser can be confused. Always use the $\vert x \vert$ notation with explicit LaTeX delimiters, or spell out "absolute value" in plain text.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Scale Calculations

### Derivation 1: Drawing Length for Reduction Scale

**Given:**
- Actual length of shaft, $L_a = 450$ mm.
- Scale required: $1 : 10$ (i.e., $S = 1/10$).

**Step 1 — Write the scale equation:**

$$S = \frac{L_d}{L_a}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for the unknown ($L_d$):**

$$L_d = S \times L_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$L_d = \frac{1}{10} \times 450$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication:**

$$L_d = 45 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft must be drawn at $45$ mm on paper.

---

### Derivation 2: Actual Length from Enlarged Drawing

**Given:**
- Length measured on a drawing, $L_d = 80$ mm.
- Drawing was prepared at scale $5:1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the scale ratio:**

$$S = 5:1 \implies S = \frac{5}{1} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse scale formula:**

$$L_a = \frac{L_d}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$L_a = \frac{80}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$L_a = 16 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The actual component measures $16$ mm.

---

### Derivation 3: Tolerance Stack-Up with GD&T Position

**Given:**
- A hole with nominal diameter $D = 20$ mm.
- Position tolerance: $\phi 0.3$ mm at MMC.
- Hole size: $20.0$ to $20.2$ mm.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum material condition (MMC) of the hole.**

For a hole, MMC is the **smallest** size (most material around the hole).

$$D_{MMC} = 20.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the virtual condition (VC) of the hole.**

The virtual condition is the worst-case boundary the part presents to its mating part:

$$VC_{hole} = D_{MMC} - \text{position tolerance}$$

$$VC_{hole} = 20.0 - 0.3 = 19.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

Any mating shaft larger than $19.7$ mm will interfere even at the worst-case tolerance limit. This is the **functional diameter** for assembly clearance.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Line Types

| Line Type | Thickness | Pattern (approx) | Termination | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Visible | Thick ($0.6$ mm) | Continuous | None (runs to corners) | 1st |
| Hidden | Thin ($0.3$ mm) | Short dashes ($2$ mm dash, $1$ mm gap) | Rounded (often at corners) | 2nd |
| Center | Thin | Long-short-short ($20$-$3$-$3$ mm cycle) | Extends $\sim 3$ mm beyond | 3rd |
| Section | Thick | Continuous with arrows | Arrowheads at both ends | Special |
| Hatching | Thin | Continuous, parallel, $45^{\circ}$ | Touches outline | Special |
| Dimension | Thin | Continuous | Arrowheads or oblique | Special |
| Extension | Thin | Continuous | Gap of $1.5$ mm from feature | Special |
| Break (short) | Thick | Freehand, irregular | Freehand | Special |
| Break (long) | Thick | Straight with zig-zag | Two zigzags | Special |
| Phantom | Thin | Long-dash-short-short | Extends beyond | Last |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conflict Resolution Rule:** When two line types overlap at the same location, the higher-priority line is drawn continuously and the lower-priority line is broken (e.g., visible line crosses a center line continuously, the center line is broken at the intersection).

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis — Creating a Sectional View

**Scenario:** You are given the orthographic views of a stepped shaft with a transverse hole, and asked to draw a full section.

**Step 1 — Identify the cutting plane.**
Choose a plane that passes through the axis of symmetry and the central hole.

**Step 2 — Draw the cutting plane line.**
Draw a thick line (with arrows) across the view, labeled "A" at both ends.

**Step 3 — Visualize the cut.**
Imagine the front half of the part is removed. The remaining half shows the cut material.

**Step 4 — Project the section view.**
Project perpendicular from the cutting plane to draw the sectional view in a new position.

**Step 5 — Apply hatching.**
Hatch the cut material at $45^{\circ}$ with thin continuous lines, spaced $\approx 2$ to $3$ mm apart.

**Step 6 — Add visible internal features.**
Draw the visible edges (now exposed) of the hole and any internal features with thick lines.

**Step 7 — Add hidden lines (only if necessary).**
Do not add hidden lines in a section view unless they are essential for clarity.

**Step 8 — Label the section.**
Write the section title (e.g., "SECTION A-A") directly above the view.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis — Applying a Position Tolerance

**Scenario:** A plate has a $10$ mm hole located by GD&T with position tolerance $\phi 0.2$ mm at MMC relative to datums A and B.

**Step 1 — Read the feature control frame.**

$$\vert \phi 0.2 \vert \ M \ \vert A \vert B \vert$$

This means: position tolerance of $0.2$ mm, MMC modifier, with datums A (primary) and B (secondary).

**Step 2 — Interpret MMC bonus.**

If the hole is at LMC (largest size, e.g., $10.1$ mm), the bonus tolerance equals:

$$\text{Bonus} = \text{Actual size} - \text{MMC size} = 10.1 - 10.0 = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total allowable position tolerance:**

$$\text{Total tolerance} = \text{Stated tolerance} + \text{Bonus}$$

$$\text{Total} = 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Verify clearance with mating part.**

Use the virtual condition formula:

$$VC = MMC - \text{Total tolerance} = 10.0 - 0.3 = 9.7 \text{ mm}$$

This is the smallest "effective" hole boundary, used for mating shaft design.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix — Traditional vs GD&T Tolerancing

| Aspect | Traditional ($\pm$) | GD&T |
|---|---|---|
| **Tolerances** | Equal in all directions | Can be unequal, directional |
| **Datum reference** | Implied by view | Explicitly stated |
| **Bonus tolerance** | Not available | Available with MMC/LMC |
| **Inspection method** | Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) | Functional gauging, CMM |
| **Communication** | Numerical only | Symbolic (universal) |
| **Cost impact** | Higher scrap rate | Lower scrap, higher design clarity |
| **Software support** | Universal | CAD-integrated (SolidWorks, NX) |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Workflow for a "Simple 2D Sketch" Lab Task

**Step 1 — Plan the geometry.**
Decide the views required (front, top, side, isometric).

**Step 2 — Select the scale.**
Choose a scale that fits the drawing sheet (A4, A3, A2) without crowding.

**Step 3 — Draw construction lines.**
Use faint lines to mark centers, symmetry axes, and boundary boxes.

**Step 4 — Sketch the views.**
Block out each view in proper proportion using visible thick lines.

**Step 5 — Add center lines.**
For all circular features, holes, and symmetric bodies.

**Step 6 — Apply dimensions.**
Use the aligned or unidirectional system. Place dimensions outside the view.

**Step 7 — Add section views (if asked).**
Identify the cutting plane, draw the section, and apply hatching.

**Step 8 — Apply GD&T (if asked).**
Add datums, feature control frames, and datum identifiers.

**Step 9 — Title block and border.**
Include drawing number, scale, units, name, date, and tolerances.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of BIS Codes for Machine Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BIS[BIS Bureau of Indian Standards]
    BIS --> IS696[IS 696 1972 General Engineering Drawing]
    BIS --> IS2709[IS 2709 1981 Dimensioning Practice]
    BIS --> IS9190[IS 9190 1979 Limits and Fits]
    BIS --> IS8000[IS 8000 GPS and GDandT Base]
    BIS --> IS11669[IS 11669 Surface Texture]
    BIS --> IS10714[IS 10714 Simplified Representation]
    IS696 --> Lines[Types of Lines]
    IS696 --> Proj[Orthographic Projection]
    IS696 --> Sect[Sectional Views]
    IS2709 --> Dim[Dimensioning Rules]
    IS2709 --> Lead[Leader and Extension Lines]
    IS2709 --> Sym[Dimensioning Symbols]
    IS9190 --> Fit[Fit Systems Clearance Transition Interference]
    IS8000 --> GDT[GDandT Form Orientation Location Profile Runout]
    GDT --> Datum[Datums and Feature Control Frames]
    Datum --> MMC[MMC and LMC Modifiers]
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Line Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify the feature to be drawn]
    Start --> Q1{Is the edge directly visible to the observer?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Visible[Draw Visible Thick Line]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the edge hidden behind a surface?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Hidden[Draw Hidden Dashed Line]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the line an axis of symmetry or center of a hole?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Center[Draw Center Line Long Dash Short Dash]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is this the path of a cutting plane?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Section[Draw Section Line with Arrows]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is this a cut surface in a section view?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Hatch[Draw Hatching at 45 Degrees]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Is this used to show a dimension value?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Dim[Draw Dimension Line with Arrowheads]
    Q6 -- No --> Const[Draw Construction Line Thin Faint]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Dimension

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extension Lines] --> B[Feature Boundary]
    B --> C[Dimension Line with Arrows]
    C --> D[Numerical Value in mm]
    D --> E[Dimension Text Placement Center or Above]
    C --> F[Leader Line for Notes]
    F --> G[Note or Diameter Symbol]
    style A fill:#e6f3ff
    style C fill:#fff0e6
    style D fill:#e6ffe6
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — The 14 GD&T Symbols Grouped by Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GDT[GDandT 14 Symbols ASME Y14.5]
    GDT --> Form[Form Tolerances 4 symbols]
    GDT --> Orient[Orientation Tolerances 3 symbols]
    GDT --> Loc[Location Tolerances 3 symbols]
    GDT --> Prof[Profile Tolerances 2 symbols]
    GDT --> Run[Runout Tolerances 2 symbols]
    Form --> F1[Straightness]
    Form --> F2[Flatness]
    Form --> F3[Circularity]
    Form --> F4[Cylindricity]
    Orient --> O1[Parallelism]
    Orient --> O2[Perpendicularity]
    Orient --> O3[Angularity]
    Loc --> L1[Position]
    Loc --> L2[Concentricity Coaxiality]
    Loc --> L3[Symmetry]
    Prof --> P1[Profile of a Line]
    Prof --> P2[Profile of a Surface]
    Run --> R1[Circular Runout]
    Run --> R2[Total Runout]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Sectional View Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sectional Views]
    S --> Full[Full Section Cutting plane passes through whole object]
    S --> Half[Half Section Symmetrical parts one half cut one half external]
    S --> Offset[Offset Section Cutting plane bent to pass through multiple features]
    S --> Aligned[Aligned Section Features rotated to align with cutting plane]
    S --> Removed[Removed Section Section drawn outside the main view]
    S --> Revolved[Revolved Section Section rotated 90 degrees on the view itself]
    S --> Partial[Partial or Broken Section Local reveal only]
```

---

## 4.6 Process Flow — Creating a Simple 2D CAD Sketch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Open CAD Software and Select 2D Sketch Plane]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2 Draw Reference Geometry Centerlines and Construction Lines]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Apply Geometric Relations Symmetric Horizontal Vertical Coincident]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Add Dimensions to Fully Define the Sketch]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Verify Sketch Status Fully Defined Under Defined or Over Defined]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Apply BIS Conventions Visible Hidden Center Section Lines]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Add GDandT Symbols Datums and Feature Control Frames]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Fill Title Block with Scale Units Name Date and Projection Symbol]
    P8 --> Done[Drawing Complete Ready for Plot or Print]
```

---

## 4.7 Block Diagram — Scale Categories in Machine Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Scale[Drawing Scales]
    Scale --> Red[Reduction Scale 1 is to N where N greater than 1]
    Scale --> Full[Full Size Scale 1 is to 1]
    Scale --> Enl[Enlargement Scale N is to 1 where N greater than 1]
    Red --> R1[1 to 2]
    Red --> R2[1 to 5]
    Red --> R3[1 to 10]
    Red --> R4[1 to 20]
    Red --> R5[1 to 50]
    Red --> R6[1 to 100]
    Enl --> E1[2 to 1]
    Enl --> E2[5 to 1]
    Enl --> E3[10 to 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**List and sketch any four types of lines used in engineering drawing as per IS 696, stating their specific applications.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 696 : 1972**, the four principal types of lines are:

1. **Visible/Object Line** — Continuous thick line, used to represent visible edges and outlines of an object.

2. **Hidden Line** — Short, evenly spaced dashes of medium thickness, used to represent edges hidden behind a surface.

3. **Center Line** — Long dash followed by a short dash (alternating), used to mark axes of symmetry and centers of circular features.

4. **Cutting Plane Line / Section Line** — Continuous thick line with arrowheads at each end, used to indicate the location and direction of viewing of a sectional view.

| Line Type | Appearance | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Visible | Continuous thick | Outlines of visible surfaces |
| Hidden | Dashed short | Hidden edges |
| Center | Long-short dash | Axes of symmetry |
| Section | Thick with arrows | Cutting plane indication |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **center lines with section lines**. Center lines are *thin* with a long-short-long pattern. Section lines are *thick* with arrowheads. Examiners strictly deduct $0.5$ to $1$ mark for this confusion.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is GD&T? List the five categories of geometric tolerances with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T)** is a symbolic engineering language used to define and communicate the allowable variation in the geometry of a part, as standardized by **ASME Y14.5** and **ISO 1101 / IS 8000**.

The five categories are:

1. **Form Tolerances** — Control the shape of a feature. Example: **Flatness**.
2. **Orientation Tolerances** — Control the angular relationship to a datum. Example: **Perpendicularity**.
3. **Location Tolerances** — Control the position of features. Example: **Position (True Position)**.
4. **Profile Tolerances** — Control the outline of a feature. Example: **Profile of a Surface**.
5. **Run-out Tolerances** — Control variations during rotation. Example: **Circular Run-out**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is listing "Concentricity" as a *form* tolerance. Concentricity is a **location** tolerance, not a form tolerance. Examiners deduct marks for this misclassification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE full question** from a choice of two. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Explain the **BIS code of practice** for engineering drawing in India. List the relevant IS codes for lines, dimensioning, and limits and fits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A machine component has an actual length of $850$ mm. It is to be drawn on an A3 sheet using scale $1:10$. Calculate the **drawing length**. If the same component is later enlarged at $5:1$, what is the **enlarged drawing length**? Also, explain the **difference between aligned and unidirectional dimensioning systems** with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — BIS Code of Practice (7 Marks):**

The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is the national body responsible for standardizing engineering drawing practices in India. The principal IS codes are:

| IS Code | Title | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 696 : 1972** | General engineering drawing practice | Lines, projections, sections, conventions |
| **IS 2709 : 1981** | Dimensioning of technical drawings | Dimension lines, symbols, placement rules |
| **IS 9190 : 1979** | Limits and fits for engineering | Tolerance grades, fit systems |
| **IS 8000** | GPS and GD&T base | Geometrical product specifications |
| **IS 11669** | Surface texture | Roughness symbols and values |

**Importance:**
- Ensures uniform interpretation across designers, manufacturers, and inspectors.
- Eliminates ambiguity in international trade.
- Forms the legal basis for engineering contracts and quality audits.

> [!TIP]
> For full marks, also mention **IS 10714 (simplified representation)** and **IS 3232 (technical product documentation)** as supporting codes.

---

**Part (b) — Scale Calculations and Dimensioning Systems (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Drawing length at scale $1:10$:**

$$L_d = L_a \times S = 850 \times \frac{1}{10} = 85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Enlarged drawing length at scale $5:1$:**

$$L_d = L_a \times S = 850 \times 5 = 4250 \text{ mm}$$

Since $4250$ mm is impractically large, we conclude that a $5:1$ scale is *not suitable* for this $850$ mm part. (1 extra mark for practical engineering judgment.)

**Step 3 — Aligned vs Unidirectional Dimensioning:**

| System | Placement | Reading Direction | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aligned** | Numerals parallel to the dimension line | From bottom and right | Older IS practice |
| **Unidirectional** | All numerals horizontal | From the bottom only | Preferred modern CAD/BIS practice |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In the **aligned system**, numerals on inclined dimension lines may be placed either parallel to the line or broken at the line, depending on orientation. Students often forget to mention this distinction and lose $1$ to $2$ marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Internal Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Define a **sectional view**. With neat sketches, explain **full section, half section, and offset section**. State the BIS rules for hatching. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plate has a $\phi 20$ mm hole with **position tolerance $\phi 0.3$ mm at MMC** relative to datums A and B. The actual hole size produced is $20.15$ mm. Calculate the **(i) virtual condition, (ii) bonus tolerance, and (iii) total allowable positional tolerance** for this part. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Sectional Views (7 Marks):**

A **sectional view** is a drawing in which a portion of the object is shown *cut away* to reveal internal features, obtained by hypothetically slicing the object with a cutting plane and removing the front portion.

**Types:**

1. **Full Section** — Cutting plane passes entirely through the object, revealing its full internal structure.

2. **Half Section** — Used for symmetric parts; one half shows the external view, the other half shows the section.

3. **Offset Section** — Cutting plane is bent (offset) to pass through multiple important features that are not aligned in a straight line.

**BIS Rules for Hatching (IS 696):**
- Hatching lines are drawn at **$45^{\circ}$** to the principal outline of the section.
- Hatching uses **continuous thin lines**, spaced approximately **$2$ to $3$ mm** apart.
- **Different parts in an assembly** are hatched in **opposite directions** or with **different spacing** to distinguish them.
- Hatching is **not** applied to shafts, bolts, nuts, keys, pins, spokes, or webs (when cut longitudinally).

---

**Part (b) — GD&T Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Hole nominal diameter: $D = 20$ mm.
- Position tolerance: $\phi 0.3$ mm at MMC.
- Actual hole size: $20.15$ mm.

**Step 1 — Identify MMC of the hole.**

For a hole, MMC is the **smallest** size (most material around the hole).

$$D_{MMC} = 20.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the bonus tolerance.**

$$\text{Bonus} = D_{actual} - D_{MMC} = 20.15 - 20.0 = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total allowable position tolerance.**

$$\text{Total tolerance} = \text{Stated tolerance} + \text{Bonus}$$

$$\text{Total tolerance} = 0.3 + 0.15 = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the virtual condition.**

$$VC_{hole} = D_{MMC} - \text{Total tolerance}$$

$$VC_{hole} = 20.0 - 0.45 = 19.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) Virtual Condition = $19.55$ mm**
- **(ii) Bonus Tolerance = $0.15$ mm**
- **(iii) Total Allowable Positional Tolerance = $0.45$ mm**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly calculate the bonus as $D_{MMC} - D_{actual}$ (giving a negative value). The bonus is always **$D_{actual} - D_{MMC}$** for a hole and **$D_{MMC} - D_{actual}$** for a shaft. Examiners award $0$ marks if the sign convention is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

### A. BIS Codes to Memorize
- **IS 696 : 1972** — General engineering drawing.
- **IS 2709 : 1981** — Dimensioning.
- **IS 9190 : 1979** — Limits and fits.
- **IS 8000** — GD&T.
- **IS 11669** — Surface texture.
- **IS 10714** — Simplified representation.

### B. Line Types and Their Uses
- Visible = continuous thick.
- Hidden = short dashes.
- Center = long-short-long dash.
- Section = thick with arrows.
- Hatching = thin, $45^{\circ}$, $2$–$3$ mm spacing.
- Dimension = thin with arrowheads.
- Break = freehand (short) or zig-zag (long).
- Phantom = long-short-short.

### C. Dimensioning Rules
- Place outside the view.
- Read from bottom or right.
- Never repeat a dimension in two views.
- Use $\phi$ for diameter, $R$ for radius, $\square$ for square.
- Hatching at $45^{\circ}$ in mm without writing "mm".

### D. Scale Formula
$$L_d = L_a \times S \quad \text{or} \quad S = \frac{L_d}{L_a}$$

- $1:1$ = full size.
- $1:5$ = reduction (object 5× larger than drawing).
- $5:1$ = enlargement (drawing 5× larger than object).

### E. Sectional View Quick Facts
- Cutting plane is a *thick line* with arrows.
- Section view is labeled (e.g., "SECTION A-A").
- Hatching at $45^{\circ}$, thin, evenly spaced.
- Different parts → opposite hatch direction.

### F. GD&T — 14 Symbols in 5 Groups
- **Form** (4): straightness, flatness, circularity, cylindricity.
- **Orientation** (3): parallelism, perpendicularity, angularity.
- **Location** (3): position, concentricity, symmetry.
- **Profile** (2): line, surface.
- **Run-out** (2): circular, total.

### G. Key Formulas
- $\text{VC (hole)} = D_{MMC} - \text{total tolerance}$.
- $\text{VC (shaft)} = D_{MMC} + \text{total tolerance}$.
- $\text{Bonus} = \vert D_{actual} - D_{MMC} \vert$ (always positive).
- $\text{Total tolerance} = \text{Stated tolerance} + \text{Bonus}$.

### H. Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- Confusing **center lines** (thin) with **section lines** (thick).
- Forgetting the $\phi$ symbol before diameter dimensions.
- Dimensioning to hidden lines.
- Using inconsistent line thickness (always maintain 2:1 ratio).
- Using $\pm$ tolerances when GD&T is required (or vice versa).
- Wrong sign convention in VC calculation.
- Hatching in the *same direction* for adjacent assembly parts.

### I. CAD Workflow Reminder
1. Set sheet size and scale.
2. Draw construction lines.
3. Block out views in proper proportion.
4. Add visible, hidden, and center lines.
5. Dimension all features.
6. Add section views if required.
7. Apply GD&T symbols and datums.
8. Fill the title block completely.

> **Final Tip:** When in doubt, **refer to IS 696** for any line-type or projection convention question. KTU examiners expect BIS-faithful answers, not "textbook shortcuts".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 2D Drafting of Rigid Shaft Couplings

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED MACHINE DRAWING & MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2D Drafting of Rigid Shaft Couplings (Flange Coupling, Protected Flange Coupling)

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid Shaft Coupling (KTU 2024 Definition):** A rigid shaft coupling is a **permanent, non-flexible mechanical device** used to connect two coaxial shafts for the purpose of transmitting **power (torque)** and motion from a driving shaft to a driven shaft, while maintaining **perfect alignment** between them. It cannot accommodate any angular, parallel, or axial misalignment.

Within the rigid coupling family, the **Flange Coupling** (also known as **Muff Coupling** or **Unprotected Flange Coupling**) and its variant **Protected Flange Coupling** are the most commonly drafted assemblies in KTU laboratory examinations because they combine fundamental machine elements — **shaft, key, bolt, nut, flange (half), and sleeve** — into a single 2D assembly drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL307 — Module Experiment 2):** The student is expected to draft the **2D orthographic assembly drawing** of the given coupling using any standard CAD package (AutoCAD / SolidWorks / Fusion 360), generate the required sectional views (full section / half section), apply BIS SP:46 dimensioning conventions, and finally produce a **part drawing** of at least one component.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two railway coaches coupled together with a **rigid metal bracket** and **bolts**. The bracket does not bend, twist, or stretch — the two coaches are locked into one straight line and move together as a single rigid unit. This bracket, in the mechanical world, is called a **flange coupling**.

Now imagine the same arrangement, but this time the bracket's edges are wrapped in a protective metal cover (a **flange guard** or **protective ring**) so that fingers, clothes, or dust cannot touch the rotating bolts while the shaft spins. That variant is the **Protected Flange Coupling**.

**Real-world mapping:**
| Mechanical Element | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Driving Shaft + Driven Shaft | Two ends of a long rotating rod (e.g., motor shaft → pump shaft) |
| Flange (Cast Iron Half) | The two coach coupling brackets bolted face-to-face |
| Bolts + Nuts | The bolts joining the brackets tightly |
| Key + Keyway | The "lock" preventing the shaft from slipping inside the flange |
| Sleeve / Muff | The cylindrical hub that grips the shaft |
| Protective Flange | The safety cover shrouding the bolts |

### 1.3 Classification of Rigid Couplings (for exam context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Classification:**

**Rigid Couplings** are sub-divided into:
1. **Sleeve / Muff Coupling** — A simple hollow cylinder (sleeve) slipped over the two shaft ends and pinned/keyed.
2. **Clamp or Split-Muff Coupling** — The sleeve is split longitudinally and clamped using bolts.
3. **Flange Coupling (Unprotected / Protected)** — Two separate flanges are keyed to the two shafts respectively and bolted together face-to-face. This is the **principal drafting topic of Module Experiment 2**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Flange Coupling — Bullet Logic Breakdown

A flange coupling assembly consists of **six primary components**. The KTU examiner expects every student to label all six in the assembly drawing.

**1. Shaft (Driving & Driven)**
- **Why:** Acts as the rotating member transmitting torque.
- **How:** The shaft is the parent element onto which the flange is keyed.
- Drafting note: Drawn as a **horizontal cylinder** with the standard convention of a single solid line for the outline + hidden lines for the inner surface (when not sectioned).

**2. Flange (Cast Iron) — 2 Nos.**
- **Why:** The flange is the torque-transmitting disc bolted to its mating flange.
- **How:** One flange is keyed to each shaft; the two flanges are then bolted together using **4 to 6 bolts** distributed uniformly on a **bolt circle (P.C.D.)**.
- Drafting note: The flange has three distinct diameters — **outer diameter (D)**, **bolt circle diameter (D₁)** and **hub diameter (D₂)**. The hub length equals the sleeve length.

**3. Key (Parallel Key)**
- **Why:** Prevents relative rotation between the shaft and the flange.
- **How:** Sits in the keyway machined into both shaft and flange hub.

**4. Sleeve (Muff)**
- **Why:** Strengthens the hub region of the flange.
- **How:** A cylindrical extension integrated with the flange casting that surrounds the shaft for a length equal to the key length + shaft entry.

**5. Bolts, Nuts & Lock Washers**
- **Why:** Clamp the two flanges face-to-face to transmit torque through friction + shear in the bolts.
- **How:** Standard **hexagonal head bolts** are inserted from one side; nuts (with spring washers) are tightened on the opposite side.

**6. Protective Flange / Guard Ring (only in Protected Flange Coupling)**
- **Why:** Safety — covers the protruding bolt heads/nuts so that operators are not injured by the rotating assembly.
- **How:** A circular cover ring fastened by small **set screws** to one of the flanges.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Proportions of a Flange Coupling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proportional Design Equations (used when dimensions are NOT given in the question):**
> In KTU exams, if the shaft diameter **d** is given, all other dimensions are derived using the **standard proportional formulas** listed below. The examiner's valuation key explicitly awards marks for these.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer diameter of flange, } D &= 2 \cdot d + 50 \text{ mm (approx.)} \\[4pt]
\text{Bolt Circle Diameter (P.C.D.), } D_1 &= \dfrac{3 \cdot D}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Hub diameter (sleeve OD), } D_2 &= 1.5 \cdot d + 12.5 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Hub / Sleeve length, } L &= 1.5 \cdot d + 12.5 \text{ mm (same as } D_2 \text{ numerically)} \\[4pt]
\text{Flange thickness, } t_f &= 0.5 \cdot d \\[4pt]
\text{Bolt diameter, } d_b &= 0.5 \cdot d \\[4pt]
\text{Number of bolts, } n &= 4 \text{ (for } d \le 40 \text{ mm), } 6 \text{ (for } d > 40 \text{ mm)} \\[4pt]
\text{Key cross-section (width } \times \text{ height)} &= \dfrac{d}{4} \times \dfrac{d}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3 Tabulated Cheat Sheet (KTU-Ready)

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula in terms of shaft dia. *d* | Typical Numerical Example (*d* = 40 mm) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter | *d* | Given | 40 | mm |
| Outer diameter of flange | *D* | 2*d* + 50 (or 3*d*) | 130 | mm |
| Bolt Circle Diameter | *D₁* | 1.5 *D* or 3*d*/2 + *d* | 200 | mm |
| Hub / Sleeve OD | *D₂* | 1.5 *d* + 12.5 | 72.5 | mm |
| Hub / Sleeve length | *L* | 1.5 *d* + 12.5 | 72.5 | mm |
| Flange thickness | *t_f* | 0.5 *d* | 20 | mm |
| Bolt diameter | *d_b* | 0.5 *d* | 20 | mm |
| Key width × height | *w* × *h* | *d*/4 × *d*/6 | 10 × 7 | mm |
| Number of bolts | *n* | 4 or 6 | 4 | nos. |
| Protective ring OD | *D₃* | *D* + 20 | 150 | mm |
| Protective ring thickness | *t_p* | 0.25 *d* | 10 | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** When the question provides **explicit dimensions**, those **override** the proportional formulas. The formulas above are only used when the problem states *"design and draw"* OR *"assume suitable proportions"*.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Pumps and Compressors:** Motor-to-pump shaft connection in centrifugal pumps.
- **Machine Tools:** Spindle-to-leadscrew connections in lathes and milling machines.
- **Power Transmission Lines:** Line shafting in factories where two sections of shafting must be joined.
- **Marine Propulsion:** Shaft sections of propeller shafts joined by flanges.
- **Industrial Fans and Blowers:** Motor-to-impeller coupling.

The drafting skill acquired here is **directly transferable** to drafting of **pipe flanges** (ASME B16.5), **pressure vessel flanges**, and **gear box housings**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Drafting Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 KTU Sheet Layout Specification

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Drawing Sheet (A2 size — 594 × 420 mm):**
> - **Title block:** Bottom-right corner, 170 × 65 mm.
> - **Border:** Inner border 10 mm from edges.
> - **Zone markings:** A–D horizontally and 1–4 vertically (per BIS SP:46).
> - **Drawing scale:** 1:1 for assembly, 1:2 for part drawing (if components are large).

### 3.2 Required Views for a Flange Coupling Assembly (KTU Valuation Key)

| View No. | View Type | Purpose | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Front View — Full Sectional** | Shows internal engagement of key, hub, bolt | **Major (5 marks)** |
| 2 | **Side / End View (without section)** | Shows bolt circle, number of bolts, keyway position | **Major (4 marks)** |
| 3 | **Top View / Half Section of one half** | Shows flange thickness, protective ring (if any) | **Minor (3 marks)** |
| 4 | **Part Drawing of one component** | Usually the **flange** OR the **key** — full dimensional drawing | **Major (5 marks)** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step 2D Drafting Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

**Phase 1 — Setting up the CAD Environment**

**Step 1:** Open the CAD package (AutoCAD 2D / SolidWorks 2D Sketch / Fusion 360 Sketch).
**Step 2:** Set **units to millimetres (mm)**, **decimal**, **precision 0.00**.
**Step 3:** Set **drawing limits** to A2 size: *594 × 420 mm* (or A3: 420 × 297 mm if permitted).
**Step 4:** Activate layers: `01-Centerline`, `02-Visible`, `03-Hidden`, `04-Section-Hatch`, `05-Dimension`, `06-Text`.

**Phase 2 — Drafting the Front View (Full Sectional)**

**Step 5:** Draw the **horizontal centerline** of length approximately `L_assembly = 2*L + 2*t_f`.
**Step 6:** Construct the **shaft** as a horizontal rectangle of height = *d* (use `RECTANGLE` command or line segments).
**Step 7:** Construct the **sleeve/hub** of height *D₂* and length *L*, symmetric about the centerline. Use the formula:

$$
\text{Half-height of hub} = \dfrac{D_2}{2} = \dfrac{1.5d + 12.5}{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Construct the **flange disc** of height *D* and thickness *t_f*, abutting the hub. The flange's left and right outlines are drawn parallel to the centerline at distance:

$$
y = \pm \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 9:** Draw the **second flange + shaft** mirror-symmetrically on the right side of the first flange. The two flanges meet face-to-face along a **vertical joint line** at the centerline's midpoint.

**Step 10:** Insert the **bolt** representation. Since this is a **full section**, the bolt shank appears as a rectangle of width *d_b*; the bolt head appears as a small clearance gap on the non-sectioned half (since BIS convention allows not sectioning standard fasteners like bolts, nuts, rivets, keys, pins).

**Step 11:** Insert the **key** as a rectangle of width *w* = *d*/4 and height *h* = *d*/6, positioned at the **top of the shaft** (12 o'clock position by convention).

**Step 12:** Apply **sectional hatching** at 45° to the cut metal portions:
- Shaft hatching (in section)
- Flange hatching (in section)
- Hub/sleeve hatching
- Do **NOT** hatch the bolt, nut, key (as per BIS conventions).

**Phase 3 — Drafting the Side / End View**

**Step 13:** Project the **bolt circle** from the front view. Diameter of bolt circle:

$$
D_1 = \dfrac{3D}{2}
$$

**Step 14:** Draw four (or six) **bolt holes** of diameter *d_b* + clearance (≈ 1 mm extra) on the bolt circle, spaced at equal angular intervals:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{360°}{n}
$$

For *n* = 4: $\theta$ = 90° (bolts at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°).
For *n* = 6: $\theta$ = 60°.

**Step 15:** Draw the **outer flange circle** (radius = *D*/2).
**Step 16:** Draw the **hub circle** (radius = *D₂*/2).
**Step 17:** Show the **keyway** as a small rectangular notch on the hub circle at the 12 o'clock (top) position.

**Phase 4 — Drafting the Top View**

**Step 18:** Project from the front view. The top view appears as a rectangle showing:
- The **flange outline** (rectangle of width = *t_f* × 2 + small clearance, height = *D*).
- The **bolt heads** as small hexagons (or circles if simplified).
- The **protective ring** as an extended outline beyond the flange outer edge if it is a **Protected Flange Coupling**.

**Phase 5 — Dimensioning (BIS SP:46 Standards)**

**Step 19:** Apply dimensions systematically:
- **Overall length** of assembly → outer-most dimension.
- **Shaft diameter *d*** → on the shaft portion.
- **Flange OD *D*** and **flange thickness *t_f*** → on the front view.
- **Hub length *L*** → on the front view.
- **Bolt circle diameter *D₁*** → on the side view (with diameter symbol ⌀).
- **Bolt diameter *d_b*** and **number of bolts *n*** → note on the side view.
- **Key dimensions** *w × h × L_key* → leader line.

**Step 20:** Add the **title block** with: Drawing title, scale, units, drawn by, checked by, date, sheet number, college code.

### 3.4 Modification Procedure — Converting Unprotected to Protected Flange Coupling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Conversion Logic (for the Protected variant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} & \quad \text{Add a protective ring (guard ring) of OD } D_3 = D + 20 \text{ mm and thickness } t_p = 0.25 d. \\[4pt]
\text{Step B:} & \quad \text{Position the ring on one flange (conventionally the driving shaft side).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step C:} & \quad \text{Fasten it using 3 or 4 small set screws (diameter } d_s = 0.2 d \text{) located on the bolt circle.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step D:} & \quad \text{In the side view, draw the protective ring as a dashed concentric circle (since it is on the far side of the flange face).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step E:} & \quad \text{In the front view section, hatch the protective ring portion and show the set screws as small rectangles.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Bill of Materials (BOM) Format

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU expects a 6-row BOM in the title block:**

| Item No. | Description | Material | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft | Mild Steel (EN8) | 2 |
| 2 | Flange | Cast Iron (FG200) | 2 |
| 3 | Key | Mild Steel (EN8) | 2 |
| 4 | Hexagonal Bolt M{d_b} | High-Tensile Steel (8.8 grade) | 4 |
| 5 | Hexagonal Nut M{d_b} | High-Tensile Steel (8.8 grade) | 4 |
| 6 | Spring Washer | Spring Steel | 4 |
| 7 *(only for Protected)* | Protective Flange Ring | Cast Iron (FG200) | 1 |
| 8 *(only for Protected)* | Set Screw M{d_s} | Mild Steel | 3 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Protected Flange Coupling**. List any **two advantages** of using a protective ring.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Construction:** A protected flange coupling consists of two cast iron flanges keyed to the driving and driven shafts respectively. The flanges are bolted together face-to-face using four (or six) hexagonal bolts. A **protective ring (flange guard)** of slightly larger diameter than the flange is mounted on one flange using set screws, completely covering the bolt heads and nuts.
>
> **Working:** Torque from the driving shaft is transmitted through the key to its flange → then through the friction and bolt shear at the joint → to the second flange → through its key → to the driven shaft. The protective ring does not transmit torque; it is purely a safety device.
>
> **Two Advantages:**
> 1. **Operator Safety:** The rotating bolts are completely shielded, preventing entanglement of clothing, hair, or fingers.
> 2. **Dust Prevention:** The protective ring keeps foreign particles from entering the bolt clearance gaps, increasing the assembly's service life.

*(Award 1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for advantages — per KTU valuation key.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series II]**
*Differentiate between a **flange coupling** and a **protected flange coupling**. Mention **two applications** of rigid flange couplings.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Flange Coupling (Unprotected) | Protected Flange Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| Protective Ring | Absent | Present — covers bolt heads |
| Operator Safety | Low | High |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Application | Internal line shafting | Open machinery, accessible drives |

**Two Applications:**
1. Connecting the motor shaft to a centrifugal pump.
2. Coupling line shafts in industrial workshops and textile mills.

*(1 mark for table, 1 mark for differences, 1 mark for applications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Not sectioning the assembly** → Lose up to 3 marks. A *full-section front view* is **mandatory** for a flange coupling in KTU exams.
> 2. **Hatching the bolts, nuts, and keys** → Lose 2 marks. As per BIS SP:46, **standard fasteners are never hatched** in sectional views.
> 3. **Missing the bolt circle dimension *D₁*** → Lose 1 mark. KTU examiners specifically look for the PCD (Pitch Circle Diameter) on the side view.
> 4. **No title block or BOM** → Lose 2 marks. These are compulsory per KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Wrong line types** → Lose 1 mark. Centerlines must be long-dash short-dash; hidden lines must be short dashes; visible lines must be continuous thick.
> 6. **Missing part drawing** → Lose up to 5 marks. The "part drawing of any one component" sub-question is **separately evaluated**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length KTU Module Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series I]**

> A **Flange Coupling** is used to connect two shafts of diameter **d = 50 mm**. Design the coupling using standard proportions and:
> **(a)** Draw the **2D orthographic assembly drawing** (front view in full section + side view) to a suitable scale, applying BIS dimensioning standards. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Draw the **part drawing of the flange** with all dimensions and tolerances. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Calculated Dimensions (using proportional formulas):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 50 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(given)} \\[4pt]
D &= 2d + 50 = 2(50) + 50 = 150 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
D_1 &= \dfrac{3D}{2} = \dfrac{3(150)}{2} = 225 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
D_2 &= 1.5d + 12.5 = 1.5(50) + 12.5 = 87.5 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
L &= D_2 = 87.5 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
t_f &= 0.5d = 0.5(50) = 25 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d_b &= 0.5d = 0.5(50) = 25 \text{ mm (use M24 bolt as nearest standard)} \\[4pt]
n &= 6 \quad (\text{since } d > 40 \text{ mm}) \\[4pt]
\text{Key} &= \dfrac{d}{4} \times \dfrac{d}{6} = 12.5 \times 8.5 \text{ mm (use } 12 \times 8 \text{ standard key)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a) — Assembly Drawing Procedure (7 Marks):**

| Step | Action | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Front View in Full Section with shaft, hubs, flanges, joint, bolt, key shown correctly | 3 |
| 2 | Side / End View with bolt circle, 6 bolt holes, keyway | 2 |
| 3 | Dimensioning per BIS SP:46 (overall length, *d*, *D*, *D₁*, *d_b*, *n*, key size) | 1 |
| 4 | Title block + BOM + centerlines + correct line types | 1 |

**Part (b) — Part Drawing of the Flange (7 Marks):**

| Step | Action | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Front view of flange + side view (half section) | 2 |
| 2 | All key dimensions: *D* = 150, *D₁* = 225, *D₂* = 87.5, *t_f* = 25, keyway width = 12, keyway depth = 5 (half of *h*) | 2 |
| 3 | Surface finish symbols (Ra 3.2 on flange face, Ra 1.6 on hub bore) | 1 |
| 4 | Material specification (Cast Iron FG200) and tolerance grades | 1 |
| 5 | Title block with scale (1:2), drawn by, date | 1 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> A **Protected Flange Coupling** is required to transmit torque between two shafts of diameter **d = 40 mm**. Assume standard proportions. **(a)** Draw the **2D assembly drawing** with all three views (front section, side view, top view), showing the protective ring clearly. **(b)** Prepare the **Bill of Materials** and explain **why** the protective ring is provided.

**Solution:**

**Calculated Dimensions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 40 \text{ mm}, \quad D = 130 \text{ mm}, \quad D_1 = 195 \text{ mm}, \\[4pt]
D_2 &= 72.5 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 72.5 \text{ mm}, \quad t_f = 20 \text{ mm}, \\[4pt]
d_b &= 20 \text{ mm (use M20 bolt)}, \quad n = 4, \\[4pt]
\text{Key} &= 10 \times 7 \text{ mm}, \quad D_3 = D + 20 = 150 \text{ mm}, \\[4pt]
t_p &= 0.25d = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad d_s = 0.2d = 8 \text{ mm (set screw)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a) — Assembly Drawing (7 Marks):**

| View | Required Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Front View (Half Section) | Shaft, hub, flange, 4 bolts (heads/nuts), key, **protective ring covering bolts on one flange**, hatching at 45° | 3 |
| Side View | Bolt circle *D₁* = 195 mm, 4 bolt holes, keyway at top, dashed outline of protective ring | 2 |
| Top View | Two flanges side-by-side, protective ring as an extra band on one flange, set screws as small circles | 2 |

**Part (b) — Bill of Materials & Justification (7 Marks):**

| Item | Description | Material | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft (Driving + Driven) | Mild Steel EN8 | 2 |
| 2 | Flange | Cast Iron FG200 | 2 |
| 3 | Parallel Key 10 × 7 | Mild Steel EN8 | 2 |
| 4 | Hex Bolt M20 × 80 | 8.8 HT Steel | 4 |
| 5 | Hex Nut M20 | 8.8 HT Steel | 4 |
| 6 | Spring Washer M20 | Spring Steel | 4 |
| 7 | Protective Ring | Cast Iron FG200 | 1 |
| 8 | Set Screw M8 × 20 | Mild Steel | 3 |

*(4 marks for BOM, 3 marks for justification of protective ring — covering operator safety, dust prevention, and IS 2062 / OSHA safety compliance.)*

**Justification (Model Answer):**
The protective ring is provided to **shield the rotating bolts and nuts** from accidental contact with operators, clothing, or foreign objects. Since the entire assembly rotates at the shaft speed (often 1000–3000 rpm), exposed bolt heads can cause serious injury or entanglement. The ring is a **non-load-bearing** safety cover, fastened via set screws, and is standard practice under **IS 2062** and **OSHA machinery safety guidelines**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Rigid Couplings cannot accommodate misalignment** — only transmit torque between perfectly aligned coaxial shafts.
- **Flange Coupling = 2 flanges + 2 keys + 4 or 6 bolts + nuts + washers + 2 shafts.**
- **Protected Flange Coupling = Unprotected Flange Coupling + 1 Protective Ring + 3 or 4 Set Screws.**
- **Key dimensions = d/4 × d/6.** Standard key sizes: 10×7 (for d = 40), 12×8 (for d = 50), 14×9 (for d = 60).
- **Bolt circle diameter *D₁* = 3*D*/2.** Always dimension this on the **side view** with the diameter symbol ⌀.
- **Number of bolts:** 4 (for d ≤ 40 mm), 6 (for d > 40 mm).
- **BIS SP:46 Sectioning Rules:** Never section bolts, nuts, keys, pins, rivets, shafts (when the cut is along the axis).
- **Section Hatching:** All cast iron parts hatched at **45° with single lines spaced ~3 mm apart**. Two adjacent parts must have **opposite hatching directions** or **different spacing**.
- **Line types:** Continuous thick = visible edges; dashed = hidden edges; long-dash short-dash = centerlines; continuous thin = dimension/leader lines.
- **Dimensioning order:** Overall → component → detail (from outside to inside).
- **Always include:** Title block, BOM, scale, units (mm), drawn by / checked by, sheet number, college code.
- **Drafting Software Skills Expected (PCMEL307):** AutoCAD 2D OR SolidWorks 2D Sketch OR Fusion 360 Sketch — using layers, blocks, dimension styles, and hatching commands.
- **Mandatory Views:** Front view in full section + Side/end view + Part drawing of at least one component.
- **Default scale:** 1:1 for assembly (since dimensions are in mm), 1:2 for larger part drawings.
- **Standard text height:** 2.5 mm for dimensions, 5 mm for titles, 3.5 mm for BOM (per BIS).
- **For a 14-mark question:** approximately **45 minutes drafting + 15 minutes dimensioning + 10 minutes title block/BOM** is the KTU-recommended time split.
- **Always verify** that all dimensions, hatching, sectioning conventions, and component counts match the **KTU model answer key** before submission.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Universal Coupling — Formal Definition

A **Universal Coupling** (also called a **Hooke's Joint** or **Cardan Joint**) is a mechanical device used to transmit rotary motion between two **non-collinear intersecting shafts**, i.e., shafts whose axes lie in the same plane but intersect at an angle (typically up to **45°**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A Universal Coupling is a positive, semi-constrained mechanical joint that transmits torque and rotational motion between two misaligned shafts using a central cross-shaped member (spider) with four trunnions engaging two perpendicular yokes.*

### 1.2 Knuckle Joint — Formal Definition

A **Knuckle Joint** is a type of **pin joint** used to connect two rods which are subjected to **tensile (pulling) loads**. It allows a small angular movement between the connected rods. It consists of a **single eye**, a **double eye (fork/clevis)**, and a **tapered knuckle pin** with a head and a split pin for locking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Universal Coupling** → Transmits **ROTATION** between misaligned shafts.
> - **Knuckle Joint** → Connects two **TENSION RODS** allowing small angular swing.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### 🎯 Universal Coupling Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of Your Car"
Imagine turning the **steering wheel** of a car. The steering column (input shaft) is **not in line** with the steering shaft going to the wheels (output shaft) — they meet at a small angle. Yet, when you rotate the steering wheel, the wheels turn. The **Universal Joint** is the small cross-shaped piece hidden in the steering column that makes this magic happen. The four "arms" of the cross spin inside two perpendicular "U-shaped forks," one on each shaft. As one fork rotates, it pushes the cross's pins, which in turn push the other fork — transferring rotation despite the angle.

### 🎯 Knuckle Joint Analogy — "The Trailer Hitch"
Think of a **trailer hitch** or a **swinging gate hinge**. Two flat bars with holes need to be joined so they can carry a pulling load but also pivot slightly. You sandwich the single-eye bar between the two-eye fork, push a tapered pin (like a giant nail) through all three holes, and lock it. The joint can carry tension like a chain link and pivot like a hinge.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU labs:** When drawing these joints, students often confuse which part goes where. Remember: **Universal Coupling = Cross + 2 Yokes**; **Knuckle Joint = 1 Single Eye + 1 Double Eye + 1 Pin**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Universal Coupling | Knuckle Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Angle of Misalignment** | **Up to 45°** | Up to **90°** swing |
| **Standard Material** | Medium Carbon Steel (EN8 / EN9) | Mild Steel (MS) or Wrought Iron |
| **Pin Type** | Hardened Trunnion Pins | Taper Pin (1:50 taper) |
| **Locking Element** | Circlip / Snap Ring | Split Pin (Cotter Pin) |
| **Load Type** | Torsional (Rotary) | Tensile (Pull) |
| **KTU Typical Drawing Scale** | 1:2 or 1:1 | 1:1 or 1:2 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Coupling — Working Principle

### Components Breakdown:
1. **Two Yokes (Forks)** — Each is a rigid fork with two parallel arms, bored to receive the cross's trunnions. Mounted on the ends of the two shafts to be connected.
2. **Cross / Spider** — A central cross-shaped member (looks like a `+` sign) with four trunnions (pins) projecting from its four arms at 90° to each other.
3. **Four Bearing Needles / Bushes** — Located in the fork holes to reduce friction.
4. **Circlips** — Retain the trunnions axially.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step:
- **Step 1:** Yoke A (driving shaft) rotates. Its two trunnion holes carry two opposite arms of the cross.
- **Step 2:** As Yoke A rotates, the two opposite pins of the cross describe circles, forcing the cross to **wobble** (oscillate) about the axes of the trunnions in Yoke B.
- **Step 3:** This wobble causes Yoke B (driven shaft) to rotate, but **not at a uniform angular velocity** if the shafts are misaligned.
- **Step 4:** The angular velocity ratio between the two shafts is given by the **condition of correct gearing** (also called the **condition for constant velocity ratio** at the mean position).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"State the condition of correct gearing for a universal coupling."* — This is asked almost every exam cycle. The answer is: **The condition of correct gearing is that the driving and driven shafts must be parallel to each other, or in other words, the angle between the centre lines of the shafts must be constant.** Alternatively: **cos α = (N₂/N₁)** where N₁ and N₂ are the speeds of the shafts and α is the angle between them.

## 2.2 Knuckle Joint — Working Principle

### Components Breakdown:
1. **Single Eye Rod End** — A rod whose end is forged into a circular eye (one hole).
2. **Double Eye / Fork (Clevis) Rod End** — A rod whose end is forged into a U-shape with two coaxial holes.
3. **Knuckle Pin** — A **tapered pin** (taper 1 in 50) with a head on one end, inserted through the aligned holes of the single eye and double eye.
4. **Split Pin (Cotter Pin)** — Passed through a transverse hole at the small end of the knuckle pin to prevent it from falling out.
5. **Taper Pin Washer (Optional)** — A small washer under the split pin.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step:
- **Step 1:** The single eye of one rod is inserted **between** the two eyes of the double eye (fork) of the other rod.
- **Step 2:** The three holes are aligned coaxially.
- **Step 3:** The **tapered knuckle pin** is driven in from the head side, so the taper is in the **direction of the load** (this allows easy removal and creates a self-tightening action).
- **Step 4:** A **split pin** is inserted through the slotted end of the knuckle pin to lock it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often draw the taper of the pin in the **wrong direction**. **Rule:** The taper of the knuckle pin must always be **in the direction of the load** (i.e., pointing towards the pulling rod). This ensures the pin does not loosen during operation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | **Condition of Correct Gearing** | $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | dimensionless | Universal Coupling |
| 2 | **Taper of Knuckle Pin** | $1 \text{ in } 50$ | ratio | Knuckle Joint |
| 3 | **Pin Diameter (Knuckle Joint — empirical)** | $d_p = \dfrac{d_t}{1.5}$ to $\dfrac{d_t}{1.6}$ | mm | Knuckle Joint |
| 4 | **Eye Outer Diameter** | $D_e = 2.5 \, d_t$ to $3 \, d_t$ | mm | Knuckle Joint |
| 5 | **Eye Inner Diameter (Pin Hole)** | $d_h = d_p + \text{clearance}$ | mm | Knuckle Joint |
| 6 | **Rod Width (Tongue)** | $w = 1.5 \, d_t$ to $1.6 \, d_t$ | mm | Knuckle Joint |
| 7 | **Length of Taper Portion of Pin** | $L_t = 2 \times \text{eye thickness}$ | mm | Knuckle Joint |
| 8 | **Cross Arm Length (Spider)** | $L_c = \text{Shaft diameter} + 2 \times \text{Yoke arm thickness}$ | mm | Universal Coupling |
| 9 | **Distance between Fork Arms** | = Cross arm thickness + 2 × bearing length | mm | Universal Coupling |
| 10 | **Maximum Allowable Angle** | $\alpha_{\max} = 45°$ | degrees | Universal Coupling |

**Where:**
- $\alpha$ = Angle between driving and driven shaft axes (Universal Coupling)
- $N_1, N_2$ = Speeds of driving and driven shafts (rpm)
- $d_t$ = Diameter of the rod (Knuckle Joint)
- $d_p$ = Diameter of the knuckle pin

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Universal Couplings** are used in: 🚗 **Automobile drive shafts, machine tool spindles, rolling mills, printing machinery, and robotic actuators.** The propeller shaft of a rear-wheel-drive car typically uses **two universal joints** to handle both angular and parallel offset misalignment.
- **Knuckle Joints** are used in: 🏗️ **Connecting rods in structural tie members, railway couplings (older designs), chain links, lever mechanisms, and bridge tension members.** They are a fundamental engineering joint in **structural steelwork** and **mechanical linkages**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Drawing Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure — Assembly Drawing of a Universal Coupling

The KTU 2024 scheme expects a full **assembled sectional view** showing **half in section and half in external view** (a **half-sectional view**). The section plane passes through the axis of the cross.

### Step 1 — Gather Given Data
- Shaft diameter = **25 mm** (typical KTU problem)
- Cross arm diameter (trunnion) = **15 mm**
- Yoke arm thickness = **10 mm**
- Yoke arm length (over trunnion) = **35 mm**
- Cross centre block = **20 × 20 × 35 mm**

### Step 2 — Plan the Sheet Layout
- **Top Half of Sheet:** Orthographic assembly drawing (front view, half-sectioned) with the **section plane line (A-A)** indicated.
- **Bottom Half of Sheet:** Title block, parts list (BOM), and scale.

### Step 3 — Draw the Centre Lines
Draw the horizontal axis of the driving shaft and the axis of the driven shaft meeting at the centre. Since the angle $\alpha$ is typically small (10° to 15° in KTU problems), the two axes are drawn in the same plane, meeting at the joint centre.

### Step 4 — Construct the Cross (Spider)
- At the joint centre, draw the **cross (spider)** in section. It will appear as a **rectangle (20 × 35 mm)** with the two **trunnion holes** (Ø15) shown as hidden/sectional holes.
- The two pairs of trunnions project at 90° to each other.

### Step 5 — Construct the Yokes
- **Yoke A (Driving):** Two parallel arms straddle the vertical arm of the cross. Their holes (Ø15) align with the cross's vertical trunnions.
- **Yoke B (Driven):** Two parallel arms straddle the horizontal arm of the cross. Their holes (Ø15) align with the cross's horizontal trunnions.
- Each yoke has a **shaft portion** (Ø25) at its outer end.

### Step 6 — Apply the Section
- The **section plane** is the **vertical plane passing through the cross's vertical axis** and the **axis of the driving shaft**.
- This cuts the cross in half vertically and the driven shaft's yoke arms.
- Apply **sectional hatching (45° lines, spaced 2-3 mm apart)** to the cut materials.

### Step 7 — Apply Hatching Rules (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatching Rules per BIS (SP 46:2003):**
> 1. **Different parts** in the same section must have hatching in **opposite directions** or with **different spacing**.
> 2. **Standard spacing** = 2 to 3 mm between parallel lines.
> 3. **Hatching angle** = **45°** to the horizontal.
> 4. **Section line weights** = thin continuous lines.
> 5. **Bolts, pins, rivets, shafts, keys, and balls/rollers** are **NOT sectioned** even if the section plane passes through them longitudinally. They are shown in **external view**.

### Step 8 — Dimension the Drawing
Apply dimensions for:
- Overall length of the assembly
- Shaft diameters (Ø25)
- Trunnion diameter (Ø15)
- Cross centre block dimensions
- Yoke arm thickness
- Angle $\alpha$ between shafts

### Step 9 — Draw the Parts List (BOM)

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Material | Qty |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Driving Shaft Yoke | EN8 Steel | 1 |
| 2 | Driven Shaft Yoke | EN8 Steel | 1 |
| 3 | Cross (Spider) | EN9 Steel, Hardened | 1 |
| 4 | Trunnion Pin | Hardened Steel | 4 |
| 5 | Circlip | Spring Steel | 4 |
| 6 | Needle Bearing | — | 4 |

### Step 10 — Title Block & Scale
- Title block: *"Assembly of Universal Coupling"*, Scale: 1:1 or 1:2, Drawn by, Checked by, Date, Sheet size (A2 or A3).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure — Assembly Drawing of a Knuckle Joint

### Step 1 — Gather Given Data
- Rod diameter $d_t$ = **30 mm** (typical KTU problem)
- Pin diameter $d_p$ = **20 mm**
- Eye outer diameter $D_e$ = **75 mm**
- Eye thickness $t$ = **15 mm**
- Taper length $L_t$ = **40 mm**
- Taper ratio = **1 in 50**

### Step 2 — Plan the Sheet
- **Top Half:** Half-sectional front view of the assembly.
- **Below:** Top view (showing the pin head) and side view of the double eye alone (optional, if space permits).

### Step 3 — Draw the Centre Lines
Draw the horizontal axis of the knuckle pin. The two rods are drawn at a small angle (typically 180°, i.e., straight in line for the basic drawing).

### Step 4 — Construct the Double Eye (Fork)
- Draw a **U-shaped fork** with two parallel circular eyes.
- Each eye: Outer Ø = 75 mm, Inner Ø (pin hole) = 20 mm + clearance = **21 mm**.
- Eye thickness = 15 mm each.
- Distance between the two eyes (inside) = 15 mm + 30 mm (single eye thickness) + 15 mm = **60 mm** (centre-to-centre of holes = 80 mm).

### Step 5 — Construct the Single Eye
- A single circular eye fitting **between** the two eyes of the fork.
- Inner Ø = 21 mm, Outer Ø = 75 mm, Thickness = 30 mm (the rod diameter).

### Step 6 — Construct the Knuckle Pin
- Cylindrical head on one side.
- Tapered body going from Ø20 (large end at the head) down to Ø19.2 (small end), over a length of 40 mm.
- A transverse hole near the small end for the **split pin (Ø3 mm hole)**.
- Slot at the small end for the split pin to bend back into.

### Step 7 — Apply Section
- The **section plane** passes through the axis of the knuckle pin and the axes of both rods.
- The upper half is shown in **section**; the lower half is in **external view**.
- Apply 45° hatching to all cut parts, with **opposite directions** for adjacent parts.

### Step 8 — Parts List

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Material | Qty |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Single Eye Rod | Mild Steel | 1 |
| 2 | Double Eye (Fork) Rod | Mild Steel | 1 |
| 3 | Knuckle Pin | EN8 Steel, Hardened | 1 |
| 4 | Split Pin (Cotter Pin) | Mild Steel | 1 |

### Step 9 — Dimensions
- All diameters (Ø), thicknesses, taper length, taper ratio, and overall length of the assembly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Drawing Conventions (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Convention | Rule | KTU Mark Weightage |
|---|---|:---:|
| Section plane indication | Two long-dash short-dash lines with arrows and labels (A-A) | **1 mark** |
| Hatching direction | Adjacent parts hatched in opposite directions | **2 marks** |
| Non-sectioned items | Pins, bolts, keys shown external even if cut longitudinally | **1 mark** |
| Centre lines | Long-dash short-dash lines extending slightly beyond outlines | **1 mark** |
| Dimensioning | Aligned system preferred, Ø symbol for diameters, R for radii | **2 marks** |
| Parts list | Numbered balloons or numbered parts in tabular form | **2 marks** |
| Title block | Bottom-right corner, complete with name, scale, date | **1 mark** |
