## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Parametric Modeling and Direct (Explicit) Modeling. Which approach is preferred for designing 3D machine components and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Parametric Modeling | Direct (Explicit) Modeling |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Geometry is driven by named dimensions, constraints, and parameters. | Geometry is pushed/pulled directly on the mesh/face. |
| **Editability** | High — change a dimension, model updates. | Low — edits must be redone manually. |
| **History** | Captured in a feature tree. | No history; pure geometry. |
| **Best For** | Engineering parts, assemblies, manufacturing drawings. | Organic shapes, sculpting, art. |
| **Software** | SolidWorks, CATIA, NX, Inventor. | Blender, ZBrush, Rhino. |

**Preferred Approach:** **Parametric modeling** is preferred for 3D machine components because engineering parts require **design intent** preservation, easy modification for design changes, automatic regeneration of drawings, and traceability of every geometric decision — all of which are mandatory under the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome CO2** (Apply parametric modeling concepts to create machine components).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is a Feature Tree? List any four features that can be added after the base feature is created.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Feature Tree** (also called the *History Tree* or *Model Tree*) is a chronological, hierarchical list of every sketch, feature, and reference geometry operation applied to a 3D part, displayed in the left pane of a CAD interface. It serves as the *audit trail* of the part's construction.

**Four Secondary Features (any four of the following):**
1. **Hole Wizard** — Creates standard holes (counterbore, countersink, tap, drill).
2. **Fillet** — Rounds internal or external edges with a specified radius.
3. **Chamfer** — Bevels edges with a specified distance and angle.
4. **Shell** — Hollows out a solid body to a specified wall thickness.
5. **Rib** — Adds a thin supporting web between two faces.
6. **Draft** — Adds taper to faces for mold-release in castings.
7. **Linear Pattern** — Repeats a feature in a linear array.
8. **Circular Pattern** — Repeats a feature around an axis.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to create a **3D model of a Hexagonal Bolt (M16 × 50, IS 1364)** using a parametric CAD software. Include details of the base feature, secondary features, and the method to model the ISO metric thread. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the **across-flats ($W$) and across-corners ($s$)** dimensions of a regular hexagon. If the across-flats dimension of an M16 hex head is **24 mm**, calculate the across-corners dimension. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Part Setup:**
- Open SolidWorks (or equivalent). Select *File → New → Part*.
- Select the **Front Plane** as the active sketch plane.

**Step 2 — Shank Base Sketch:**
- Draw a circle of diameter **16 mm** centered at the **Origin** (this is the nominal diameter $d = 16$).
- Apply a *Dimension* relation locking the diameter to 16 mm.
- Exit the sketch.

**Step 3 — Extrude the Shank:**
- Use *Features → Extruded Boss/Base*.
- Set the extrusion length to **50 mm** (the bolt length per designation M16 × 50).
- Direction: Blind, mid-plane not selected.
- Click OK. The shank is now a 3D cylinder.

**Step 4 — Hex Head Sketch:**
- Select the **circular end face** of the shank (the head will grow from this face).
- Click *Sketch*. Use the *Polygon* tool to draw a hexagon inscribed in a circle of diameter **24 mm** (this is the standard $W$ for M16).
- Apply an *Equal* constraint to all 6 sides.
- Apply *Vertical* and *Horizontal* constraints to one side to fully define the hexagon.

**Step 5 — Extrude the Head:**
- Use *Extruded Boss* with depth = **10 mm** (standard head height for M16).
- Direction: Away from the shank.

**Step 6 — Chamfer the Head:**
- Select the top cylindrical edge of the head.
- Use *Chamfer* with dimensions **1.6 mm × 45°** (per IS 1364).

**Step 7 — Model the ISO Metric Thread (Coarse):**
- For M16, the standard coarse pitch $P = 2.0$ mm.
- Use *Features → Sweep* with a *Helix* profile.
- Define a helix along the shank axis with:
  - Pitch = **2.0 mm**
  - Number of revolutions = $L / P = 50 / 2.0 = 25$ turns.
- Draw a triangular thread profile (equilateral triangle with side $P = 2.0$ mm, height = $0.541 \cdot P = 1.082$ mm).
- Use *Cut-Sweep* to remove material along the helix, creating the thread.
- Alternatively, use the *Thread* feature in SolidWorks (a cosmetic thread representation) for the lab, which is acceptable per KTU valuation norms.

**Step 8 — Final Touches:**
- Apply a *Fillet* of radius **0.5 mm** at the shank-to-head junction.
- Apply a *Chamfer* of **1.0 mm × 45°** at the shank tip.
- Save as *HexBolt_M16.SLDPRT* (or .STEP for neutral format).

**Marks Distribution (7M):** Setup (1) + Shank (1) + Head (2) + Thread (2) + Final touches (1).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1:** As derived in Section 3.2, the across-corners distance of a regular hexagon is given by:

$$
s = 1.1547 \, W
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $W = 24$ mm:

$$
s = 1.1547 \times 24
$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication:

$$
s = 27.7128 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Round to engineering precision (KTU accepts 2 decimal places):

$$
\boxed{s \approx 27.71 \text{ mm}}
$$

*Verification using exact formula:*

$$
s = \frac{2W\sqrt{3}}{3} = \frac{2 \times 24 \times 1.7320508}{3} = \frac{83.138}{3} = 27.713 \text{ mm}
$$

The two methods agree, confirming the answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Boundary Representation (B-Rep)**. State and apply **Euler's formula** to verify whether the following solid is topologically valid: a cube has **8 vertices** and **12 edges**. Find the number of faces. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stepped shaft is modeled by revolving a 2D profile consisting of two rectangles: Rectangle 1 has width $r_1 = 20$ mm and length $L_1 = 60$ mm; Rectangle 2 has width $r_2 = 15$ mm and length $L_2 = 40$ mm. Using Pappus's theorem, calculate the **volume of the revolved solid**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**B-Rep Definition:** Boundary Representation is a solid modeling scheme in which a 3D object is described entirely by its boundary surfaces (faces), which in turn are bounded by edges, which terminate at vertices. The interior of the object is implicit. This is the data structure used by the **Parasolid** kernel (used by SolidWorks) and the **ACIS** kernel (used by CATIA and AutoCAD).

**Euler's Formula:**

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the known values $V = 8$, $E = 12$:

$$
8 - 12 + F = 2
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $F$:

$$
-4 + F = 2
$$

$$
F = 2 + 4
$$

**Step 3:** Final answer:

$$
\boxed{F = 6}
$$

*Verification:* A cube has 6 faces, which is correct. The solid is topologically valid.

**Note for KTU marking:** If the student writes $F - E + V = 2$ (a sign variant), it is equally correct. Full marks awarded for either form.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Rectangle 1: width $r_1 = 20$ mm, length $L_1 = 60$ mm.
- Rectangle 2: width $r_2 = 15$ mm, length $L_2 = 40$ mm.

**Step 1:** Calculate the total area of the profile.

$$
A = r_1 L_1 + r_2 L_2
$$

$$
A = (20 \times 60) + (15 \times 40)
$$

$$
A = 1200 + 600 = 1800 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the centroid distance from the axis of revolution.

For a rectangle, the centroid is at half its width from the axis. The composite centroid is the area-weighted average:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{r_1 L_1 \cdot \frac{r_1}{2} + r_2 L_2 \cdot \frac{r_2}{2}}{A}
$$

*Explanation:* The centroid of a rectangle of width $r$ is at $r/2$ from the axis. The moment of each rectangle about the axis is its area times its centroid distance.

**Step 3:** Calculate the numerator (first moment of area):

$$
M = (20 \times 60 \times 10) + (15 \times 40 \times 7.5)
$$

$$
M = 12000 + 4500 = 16500 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\bar{x}$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{16500}{1800} = 9.1667 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply Pappus's Theorem.

$$
V = 2\pi \bar{x} A
$$

$$
V = 2\pi \times 9.1667 \times 1800
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the final volume.

$$
V = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 9.1667 \times 1800
$$

$$
V = 103,672.8 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V \approx 1.037 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 103.67 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Euler's Formula:** Students frequently write $V + E - F = 2$. The correct form is $V - E + F = 2$. Marks are deducted for the wrong sign convention. **Remember: Vertices minus Edges plus Faces equals 2.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Across-Flats vs. Across-Corners:** A common error is multiplying $W$ by 2 (i.e., $s = 2W$) instead of dividing by $\sqrt{3}$. Always draw the hexagon and label the dimensions before calculating.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Thread Pitch:** When modeling an M16 bolt, students often use $P = 1.0$ mm (wrong). The correct ISO metric coarse pitch for M16 is **2.0 mm**. KTU examiners will specifically check this against IS 4218 tables.
>
> 4. **Pappus Theorem Misapplication:** The centroid must be measured from the *axis of revolution*, not from the end of the shaft. Drawing a free-body diagram of the profile with the axis clearly marked is mandatory for full marks.
>
> 5. **Missing "Fully Defined" Sketch:** A sketch with blue (under-defined) entities will cause the CAD model to fail regeneration. The feature tree will show a yellow warning icon. This is a 1-mark deduction in the KTU lab record.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **3D Machine Component Modeling** is a *feature-based, parametric* process that creates a digital solid using sequential *Sketch → Feature* operations stored in a **Feature Tree**.
- The underlying mathematical structure is **Boundary Representation (B-Rep)**, validated by **Euler's formula** $V - E + F = 2$.
- The four primary base features are **Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, and Loft**. The choice depends on whether the part is prismatic, axisymmetric, follows a path, or blends between profiles.
- For KTU lab work, always model to the **Indian Standard (IS) codes**: IS 1364 for bolts, IS 2048 for keys, IS 2708 for couplings.
- The **hexagonal head** dimension conversion is $s = 1.1547 \, W$ — a **derivable**, high-yield formula for KTU exams.
- **Pappus-Guldinus Theorem** is the official method to compute the volume of any revolved 3D part: $V = 2\pi \bar{x} A$.
- **ISO metric thread pitch** for a nominal diameter $d$ is read from tables (e.g., M16 → 2.0 mm, M20 → 2.5 mm) — memorize the common values.
- The KTU lab evaluation tests **four outcomes**: (i) correct base feature selection, (ii) fully defined sketches, (iii) clean feature tree ordering, and (iv) accurate engineering drawing generation from the 3D model.
- **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX** for absolute value notation — a plain `|` symbol breaks markdown table rendering.
- **Parametric modeling is the industry standard** — every dimension should be a *named parameter*, never a hard-coded number, so that design changes propagate automatically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 3D Assembly Modeling — Core Definitions & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL307 - COMPUTER AIDED MACHINE DRAWING & MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly Modeling (Socket and Spigot Joint, Knuckle Joint, Rigid Flange Couplings, Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling, Plummer Block, Screw Jack)

---

## 1.1 What is 3D Assembly Modeling?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
3D Assembly Modeling is the process of creating a virtual three-dimensional representation of a complete mechanical product by combining individually modeled parts with proper constraints (mates/coincident/align) that simulate real-world degrees of freedom, motion behaviour, and physical fit. In the KTU lab context, it is the parametric assembly of standard machine elements using **SolidWorks / CATIA / NX / Fusion 360** by referencing standard part libraries and enforcing design intents through bottom-up or top-down modelling strategies.

**Key Outcome (CO mapped):** The student must be able to model, constrain, and validate a working assembly, then explode it for drawing extraction and generate an orthographic bill-of-materials view.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Directive:** Every assembly must contain (i) a properly constrained main assembly file, (ii) individual part files with consistent units, (iii) a BOM table in the drawing view, and (iv) an exploded view showing part separation vectors. Missing any one of these four will attract full mark deduction during external evaluation.

## 1.2 Component-Wise Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogies)

### 1.2.1 Socket and Spigot Joint
Think of it as a **"mortar and pestle ring"** — the spigot (male end) sits inside the socket (female end) and is sealed with caulking lead or rubber. In your house, you can imagine the joint where an old cast-iron soil pipe enters a wall socket — the lip of the pipe rests on the socket shoulder and is held in place. In CAD, you model the socket as a hollow flanged cylinder and the spigot as a smaller cylinder that drops inside it with a small clearance fit (typically **0.5 mm to 2 mm** depending on pipe diameter).

> [!NOTE]
> Used for **low-pressure pipelines** (water, drainage, vent lines). Carries no thrust load — only fluid pressure containment.

### 1.2.2 Knuckle Joint
Picture a **door hinge**, but for pulling force instead of swinging — it is a forked-end mechanical linkage where two rods meet end-to-end through a **single pin**, allowing the rods to pivot through a small angle. On your bicycle, the brake rod connects to the lever using exactly this principle. In SolidWorks, you model two identical forks, one with the pin and one with a knuckle pin inserted through matching holes, with a **cotter pin** hole on the protruding pin end.

> [!NOTE]
> Used for **tie-rods, pull-rods, and brake linkages** of locomotives, presses, and steering systems. Resists axial tensile load only.

### 1.2.3 Rigid Flange Coupling
Imagine two **circular metal plates bolted back-to-back** on a motorcycle shaft — that is a flange coupling. The flanges are keyed to their respective shafts, then bolted together with **4 to 8 bolts** evenly spaced on a pitch circle. In CAD, you model two identical flanges, a key, two shafts, and standard hex bolts through concentric bolt holes. There is **zero misalignment tolerance** — it is rigid.

> [!NOTE]
> Used where **shaft alignment is precise** and torque transmission must be positive (no slip). Common in **machine tool spindles, pump drives, compressors**.

### 1.2.4 Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling
This is the **"shock-absorber sibling"** of the rigid flange coupling — instead of direct metal-to-metal contact between flanges, **rubber/leather bushes** are mounted over pins. Think of the rubber bushings in your car's propeller shaft — they absorb small misalignments and vibrations. In CAD, the bushes are modeled as **sleeve-like cylindrical liners** with holes for the pins, sandwiched between the two flange hubs.

> [!NOTE]
> Tolerates **angular misalignment up to 1°** and **parallel misalignment up to 0.2 mm**. Used in **electric motors, generators, light machinery**.

### 1.2.5 Plummer Block (Pedestal Bearing)
A **"pillow for the shaft"** — literally a cast-iron or steel pillow-shaped housing that supports a rotating shaft through a journal bearing. Open the cover, drop in the shaft with bearings, and bolt it shut. In CAD, you model the **base, cap (top), two bearing halves, two bolts for cap, and two holding-down bolts** for the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> Supports **radial load only** (not axial thrust). Common in **conveyor shafts, line shafts, intermediate support in long shaft systems**.

### 1.2.6 Screw Jack
The classic **"car lift in your garage"** — rotate the handle, the threaded screw turns inside a nut, and a cup rises up to lift the load. The **mechanical advantage** is enormous because of the very fine thread helix angle. In CAD, you model the **body (with internal nut bore), the screw/leadscrew, the cup (with cup-pin slot), and the handle (with hinge pin)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Used for **lifting automobiles, machine mounting, structural elevation**. The handle end has a square head engaging a tommy bar.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants (KTU 2024 Lab Manual)

The following constants are universally used in the KTU dimensioning scheme for all six assemblies. They appear in the **KTU Standard Data Book (M.D.S. Data Book)** and should be memorized.

| Constant | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Standard bolt size (small machines) | M | **M8, M10, M12, M16** |
| Number of bolts in rigid flange | n | **4 or 6** |
| Pitch circle diameter factor | $D_p / D$ | **1.5 to 2.0** |
| Knuckle pin diameter factor | $d_p / d$ | **0.5 to 0.6** |
| Socket clearance | c | **0.5 mm to 2 mm** |
| Plummer block bolt centre | — | **1.75 × bolt diameter** |
| Screw jack lead (typical) | L | **10 mm to 20 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proportional Memory Trick:** For any flange/coupling type, the outer diameter is roughly **2× to 3× the shaft diameter**, and the hub length is roughly **1.5× the shaft diameter**. This avoids re-calculating from scratch during the 3-hour lab exam.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

## 2.1 Assembly Modeling Methodology (Theory)

Every 3D assembly in the KTU lab follows a four-stage workflow. This is the **"Why and How"** behind the steps you take in SolidWorks/CATIA.

### Stage 1 — **Bottom-Up vs Top-Down Decision**
- **Bottom-up**: All parts are modeled as separate files, then assembled. Used when parts are **standard/procured items** (bolts, nuts, bearings). This is the **KTU-recommended default**.
- **Top-down**: The assembly skeleton is created first, then parts are derived from assembly-level sketches. Used for **highly custom geometries**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The examiner specifically checks whether you used **"Insert Part"** with external references (bottom-up) versus in-context features (top-down). Mentioning the correct strategy in your lab record gets you 2 bonus marks.

### Stage 2 — **Constraint Selection (Mate Logic)**

The mating conditions are the heart of the assembly. Use the minimum number of mates to fully constrain a part (over-constraining causes red error indicators in SolidWorks).

| Constraint Type | Function | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Coincident** | Faces touch at a point or line | Bolt head touching flange surface |
| **Concentric** | Cylindrical axes align | Pin inside fork hole |
| **Distance / Offset** | Maintains gap | Clearance in socket-spigot |
| **Angle** | Rotational offset | Handle at 90° to body in screw jack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A part with **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translational + 3 rotational) needs exactly 6 constraints to be fully fixed — but a cylindrical part only needs **4 constraints** (2 from concentric + 2 coincident for the axial face). Over-constraining produces yellow warnings and is a common KTU mistake.

### Stage 3 — **Exploded View Generation**
Create an **explode view** by selecting each component and dragging along the assembly's principal axes. KTU requires:
- **One explode line per direction**
- Explode direction aligned with the **functional separation path** (e.g., along the bolt axis for bolted assemblies)

### Stage 4 — **Drawing Extraction**
From the assembly, generate:
1. **Orthographic views** (top, front, side)
2. **Sectional view** through the centre plane
3. **BOM (Bill of Materials)** as a table
4. **Balloon callouts** (numbered circles with leader lines)

## 2.2 KTU Design Data Tables for Each Assembly

### 2.2.1 Socket and Spigot Joint — Proportions

$$
D_{socket} = D_{spigot} + 2c
$$

$$
L_{socket} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times D_{spigot}
$$

$$
L_{spigot} = L_{socket} + (1.0 \times D_{spigot})
$$

Where **c** is the clearance (typically **1 mm to 2 mm**).

### 2.2.2 Knuckle Joint — Proportions

$$
d_p = 0.5 \times d \quad \text{(pin diameter, empirical)}
$$

$$
d_e = 0.75 \times d \quad \text{(eye outer diameter)}
$$

$$
t = 0.75 \times d \quad \text{(thickness of fork)}
$$

$$
b = 1.5 \times d_p \quad \text{(fork prong width)}
$$

Where **d** is the rod diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Validation Check (must include in your record):**
> 
> $$ \tau_{pin} = \frac{4W}{\pi d_p^2} \leq 60 \text{ MPa (for mild steel)} $$
> 
> $$ \sigma_{eye} = \frac{W}{(d_e - d_p) \times t} \leq 80 \text{ MPa} $$
> 
> If W = 20 kN, d = 30 mm → d_p = 15 mm → τ_pin = 113 MPa ❌ (fails). Increase d_p to 18 mm → τ_pin = 78 MPa ✓.

### 2.2.3 Rigid Flange Coupling — Proportions

$$
D = 2 \times d \quad \text{(flange outer diameter)}
$$

$$
D_p = 1.5 \times D \quad \text{(pitch circle diameter, sometimes 1.5d... 2d)}
$$

$$
t_f = 0.5 \times d \quad \text{(flange thickness)}
$$

$$
n_{bolts} = 4 \text{ for } d \leq 40 \text{ mm, } 6 \text{ for } d > 40 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_b = \frac{D_p \times \sin(\pi/n)}{\pi} \times \text{load factor} \quad \text{(bolt diameter derived)}
$$

In lab practice, students take bolt size from standard charts: **M8 for d ≤ 25 mm, M10 for d ≤ 40 mm, M12 for d ≤ 55 mm, M16 for d ≤ 75 mm**.

### 2.2.4 Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling — Proportions

Same flange dimensions as rigid, but:
- **Bore of bush** = $d_p$ (pin diameter)
- **Bush length** = $1.5 \times d_p$
- **Bush material** = Leather / rubber / nylon (modelled as grey/elastic in CAD)
- **Number of pins** = 4 to 8

### 2.2.5 Plummer Block — Proportions

$$
L_{base} = (4 \text{ to } 5) \times d \quad \text{(bearing length)}
$$

$$
W_{base} = (2.5 \text{ to } 3) \times d \quad \text{(base width)}
$$

$$
H_{base} = 1.5 \times d \quad \text{(base height up to shaft centre)}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 2 \times d \quad \text{(with cap)}
$$

$$
\text{Cap bolt distance} = 2 \times d \quad \text{(centre-to-centre)}
$$

$$
\text{Bore in cap/base} = d + 2 \times t_{bush}
$$

Where **d** = shaft diameter and **$t_{bush}$** = bearing bush thickness (typically **5 mm to 8 mm**).

### 2.2.6 Screw Jack — Proportions

$$
d_{core} = 0.6 \times d_{screw} \quad \text{(core/root diameter of screw)}
$$

$$
L_{body} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \times d_{screw} \quad \text{(height of body above base)}
$$

$$
d_{cup} = 2 \times d_{screw} \quad \text{(cup outer diameter)}
$$

$$
t_{cup} = 0.2 \times d_{screw}
$$

$$
\text{Handle length} = (4 \text{ to } 5) \times d_{screw}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Key Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Socket clearance | $D_s = D_p + 2c$ | mm |
| 2 | Knuckle pin diameter | $d_p = 0.5d$ | mm |
| 3 | Knuckle pin shear stress | $\tau_p = 4W / (\pi d_p^2)$ | MPa |
| 4 | Knuckle eye tensile stress | $\sigma_e = W / [(d_e - d_p) \cdot t]$ | MPa |
| 5 | Flange PCD | $D_p = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) D$ | mm |
| 6 | Flange outer diameter | $D_o = (2 \text{ to } 3) d$ | mm |
| 7 | Flange thickness | $t_f = 0.5 d$ | mm |
| 8 | Plummer block base length | $L_b = (4 \text{ to } 5) d$ | mm |
| 9 | Plummer block base width | $W_b = (2.5 \text{ to } 3) d$ | mm |
| 10 | Plummer block cap bolt C-C | $C_c = 2 d$ | mm |
| 11 | Screw jack load capacity | $W = \pi \cdot d_c \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N |
| 12 | Screw jack efficiency | $\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> In screw jack formula #11, the load W depends on the **helix angle α** and the **friction angle φ**. The KTU external lab exam only requires identifying these symbols in a labelled diagram — actual numerical integration is part of design of machine elements, NOT this lab. **Do not spend time solving screw jack forces in the CAD lab.**

# Step-by-Step Assembly Modeling Workflows

## 3.1 Universal Pre-Modelling Checklist (Do This First in Lab)

Before you touch any software in the **3-hour KTU lab**, perform these steps on paper:

1. **List all parts** (e.g., Knuckle Joint = 6 parts: 2 forks, 2 rods, 1 pin, 1 cotter pin)
2. **Identify the base part** (the part that sits on the ground or is the reference for all mates)
3. **Sketch the mate skeleton** — arrows showing which face mates with which face
4. **Calculate dimensions** using the proportional formulas from Section 2
5. **Decide units** (always mm — KTU standard)
6. **Set up the folder structure**: `AssemblyName > Parts > Drawings > Assemblies`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 10-minute planning activity is **directly weighted** (5 marks) in the KTU lab record. Skipping it costs you guaranteed marks.

---

## 3.2 Socket and Spigot Joint — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Step 1: Create the Socket Part
- Start a new part file: `Socket_and_Spigot_Socket`
- On the **Front Plane**, sketch:
  - Two concentric circles: inner radius $R_i = 25$ mm, outer radius $R_o = 35$ mm
  - Two horizontal lines forming the rectangular cross-section, length $L_s = 60$ mm
- **Extrude** by 60 mm → this gives the socket tube
- Add a **flange** at one end: extrude an outer ring of width 10 mm and thickness 8 mm

### Step 2: Create the Spigot Part
- New part file: `Socket_and_Spigot_Spigot`
- Sketch: circle of radius 24.5 mm (1 mm clearance from socket inner radius)
- Extrude length = 80 mm (longer than socket to leave protrusion)

### Step 3: Create the Assembly
- Open a new Assembly file
- Insert **Socket** — set as fixed (origin alignment)
- Insert **Spigot** — apply:
  - **Concentric mate** between socket inner cylindrical face and spigot outer cylindrical face
  - **Coincident mate** between spigot end face and socket flange face (or vice versa for the desired overlap)

$$
L_{overlap} = L_s - L_{protrusion\ inside\ socket} \geq 1.5 \times D_{spigot}
$$

### Step 4: Validate
- Try to drag the spigot — it should only move along the socket axis (1 DOF left). If it moves in other directions, you missed a mate.

---

## 3.3 Knuckle Joint — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Step 1: Sketch the Eye Geometry
For a knuckle joint with rod diameter **d = 30 mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_p &= 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ mm} \\
d_e &= 0.75 \times 30 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \approx 23 \text{ mm} \\
t &= 0.75 \times 30 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \approx 23 \text{ mm} \\
b &= 1.5 \times 15 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \approx 23 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Model Each Fork
- Sketch the **eye profile**: rectangle $b \times d_e$ attached to rod section
- Add a **circular hole** of diameter $d_p = 15$ mm at the eye centre
- Add the **cotter pin slot** on top: rectangular slot of width 5 mm × depth 8 mm through the prong gap
- **Extrude** by thickness $t = 23$ mm
- For the **fork** (double eye), model two prongs separated by a slot of width $b = 23$ mm

### Step 3: Model the Pin and Cotter
- Pin: cylinder Ø15 × 70 mm (length enough to pass through both forks and project beyond)
- Cotter: tapered flat strip with a tapered slot engagement

### Step 4: Assembly Mating Sequence

1. **Insert Rod 1 (single eye)** — fix it
2. **Insert Rod 2 (fork)** — concentric between single-eye hole and fork prongs' inner faces
3. **Insert Pin** — concentric between pin outer face and fork prongs' holes; coincident face-to-face with the single-eye
4. **Insert Cotter** — through the slot in pin's protruding end

> [!TIP]
> **Critical validation check in SolidWorks:** Click **"Move Component"** and rotate one rod — the entire assembly should pivot about the pin axis only. If it wobbles or translates, your concentric mates are misaligned (off-axis by even 0.1 mm).

---

## 3.4 Rigid Flange Coupling — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Step 1: Calculate Dimensions for d = 40 mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm} \\
D_p &= 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ mm} \\
t_f &= 0.5 \times 40 = 20 \text{ mm} \\
n &= 6 \text{ bolts} \\
d_b &= \text{M10} \quad \text{(from standard chart)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Model the Flange (as a single part, mirrored later)
- Sketch on front plane:
  - Outer circle Ø80 mm
  - Hub circle Ø40 mm (matches shaft)
  - Bolt circle Ø120 mm with **6 equispaced Ø11 mm clearance holes** (1 mm clearance over M10)
  - Centre keyway slot: 12 mm × 4 mm
- **Extrude** 20 mm
- Add **counterbore** on the flange face for bolt heads (Ø18 mm × 6 mm deep) and for the nut on the opposite side

### Step 3: Model the Key
- Standard parallel key: width = **d/4** = 10 mm, height = **d/6** = 6.7 mm → use 7 mm, length = **1.5 × d** = 60 mm

### Step 4: Assembly
1. Insert **Left Flange** (fixed)
2. Insert **Shaft 1** — concentric with left flange hub, coincident face
3. Insert **Key** — coincident with keyway in shaft and keyway in flange hub
4. Insert **Right Flange** — concentric with shaft, faces coincident with left flange
5. Insert **6 Hex Bolts** — concentric with clearance holes; bolt heads sit in counterbore, threads project through to right flange
6. Insert **6 Hex Nuts** — on the protruding threads

> [!NOTE]
> In SolidWorks, you can use **"Smart Fasteners"** (Toolbox) to automatically insert all bolts and nuts once you specify the bolt size and hole pattern. This is a major time-saver in the KTU 3-hour lab.

---

## 3.5 Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Differences from Rigid Flange
1. Add **flange thickness reduction** by half (since each flange is paired with a rubber bush)
2. Model **Pins** as separate cylinders — typically **4 to 6 pins** on a smaller pitch circle (closer to hub)
3. Model **Bushes** as **hollow cylindrical sleeves**:
   - Inner Ø = pin diameter $d_p$
   - Outer Ø = flange pin-bore diameter
   - Length = bush length $L_b \approx 1.5 d_p$
4. The **mating logic**: pin is fixed in one flange's bore; bush slips over the pin; other flange's bore grips the bush's outer surface

### Mating sequence
- Pin fixed in left flange
- Bush concentric over pin
- Right flange concentric over bush
- All with proper clearance mates (offset = 0.1 mm each side, total 0.2 mm radial play)

---

## 3.6 Plummer Block — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Step 1: Base Part
- Sketch on top plane: rectangle $L_b \times W_b$ = $200 \times 120$ mm (for d = 40 mm)
- Extrude by $H_b = 60$ mm
- Add a **semicircular saddle** on top: Ø40 mm bore for the shaft (use **Cut-Extrude**)
- Add **two mounting holes** at the base for floor bolts: Ø13 mm × 2, at $0.75 \times L_b$ apart
- Add **two cap-bolt clearance holes** vertical through the base wall

### Step 2: Cap Part
- Sketch a **matching semicircle** with flanges that bolt to the base
- Extrude by 15 mm

### Step 3: Bearing Bush
- Hollow cylinder: outer Ø40 mm, inner Ø36 mm (4 mm wall thickness, representing the actual white-metal bush)
- Length = 50 mm

### Step 4: Shaft (for context, not always modelled)

### Step 5: Assembly
1. Insert Base (fixed)
2. Place bearing bushes inside the saddle (left and right halves if split)
3. Place shaft through bushes
4. Cap goes on top — concentric with the saddle bore, face-coincident
5. Cap bolts pass through cap into base — **Smart Fasteners** approach

---

## 3.7 Screw Jack — Detailed Modelling Workflow

### Step 1: Body Part
- Sketch: rectangular section with a hollow bore for the nut
- Body height $H_b = 80$ mm (for $d_{screw} = 30$ mm)
- Bore diameter = $d_{screw} + 1$ mm clearance = 31 mm
- Add **flange ribs** at the base for strength (4 ribs at 90°)
- Add **handle pivot pin** hole on the side

### Step 2: Screw (Leadscrew)
- Sketch a circle Ø30 mm
- **Helix sweep** with pitch = 10 mm (one full turn of thread) — this creates the thread profile
- Add a **square head** at the bottom of the screw for the tommy bar
- Add a **cup-pin hole** at the top to receive the cup

### Step 3: Cup Part
- Top cup shape: cylindrical with a recess for the load
- A small pin at the bottom fits into the screw's head hole (anti-rotation feature)
- Outer Ø = 60 mm, height = 20 mm

### Step 4: Handle (Tommy Bar)
- Long rod Ø12 mm, length = 150 mm
- Square socket at one end fits the screw head

### Step 5: Assembly Mating
1. Body fixed
2. Screw inside body — concentric with bore; **only rotation allowed** (constrained with coaxial + axial distance, but rotation is the free DOF we want to demonstrate)
3. Cup on top of screw — concentric with screw tip pin
4. Handle engages screw head square end

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** When asked to demonstrate the screw jack operation in SolidWorks, use **"Rotate Component"** mode on the screw — the cup should move up/down by exactly **10 mm** per revolution (lead value). If it moves by 10 mm per two revolutions, your pitch value is set incorrectly (you used pitch instead of lead).

---

## 3.8 Exploded View Generation — Universal Procedure

1. Switch to the assembly file
2. Click **"Exploded View"** toolbar button
3. Select each component one at a time
4. Set the **explode direction** along the assembly's functional separation axis:
   - Socket-Spigot: along the pipe axis
   - Knuckle: along the pin axis
   - Couplings: along the shaft axis
   - Plummer: vertical (cap lifts up)
   - Screw Jack: vertical (cup lifts up, screw rotates)
5. Set the explode distance — typically **1.5× to 2× the part's largest dimension**
6. Click **OK** to commit

## 4.2 Mating Logic Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Base Part] --> S2[Step 2: Insert Base and Fix at Origin]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Insert Mating Component 1]

    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Apply Concentric Mate]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Apply Coincident Mate]
    S5 --> S7[Apply Face Coincident for Axial Position]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8{6 DOF Constrained?}
    S8 -- No --> S3
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Insert Mating Component 2]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Mates to Component 2]
    S10 --> S11[Repeat Until All Parts Placed]
    S11 --> S12[Validate by Drag Test]
    S12 --> S13{Any Unwanted Motion?}
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Add Missing Constraint]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**
**Define "Degree of Freedom" in the context of 3D assembly modelling. How many DOFs must be constrained to fully fix a rigid body in 3D space?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Degree of Freedom (DOF) is an independent direction in which a part can move without causing constraint violation. In 3D space, a free rigid body possesses **six DOFs**: three translational (along X, Y, Z axes) and three rotational (about X, Y, Z axes). To fully fix a part in an assembly, all six DOFs must be constrained using mates such as coincident (3 translational) and concentric or angle (3 rotational). Cylindrical parts only require **four constraints** because the cylindrical symmetry inherently constrains two rotational DOFs about the cylinder axis. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**
**List any four components of a Rigid Flange Coupling and state the standard proportional formula used to determine its outer diameter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four main components of a Rigid Flange Coupling are:
1. **Two flanges** (one keyed to each shaft)
2. **Two shafts** (driver and driven)
3. **Two parallel keys** (one per shaft)
4. **Bolts and nuts** (4 to 6 sets)

Standard proportional formula: **Outer diameter D = 2 × d**, where **d** is the shaft diameter. The pitch circle diameter $D_p$ = (1.5 to 2.0) × D, and flange thickness $t_f$ = 0.5 × d. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the constructional features of a **Knuckle Joint**. Mention the standard proportional relations used in its design and list the materials typically used.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Knuckle Joint is required to transmit an axial tensile load of **W = 25 kN**. The rod diameter is **d = 35 mm**. Design the joint by calculating the pin diameter, eye outer diameter, fork thickness, and prong width. Validate the design for **shear of pin** and **tensile failure of eye** using allowable stresses **τ = 60 MPa** and **σ_t = 80 MPa** for mild steel.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Features of Knuckle Joint — [7 marks]**

**Sketch description (verbal — full marks only with diagram in record):**

A knuckle joint consists of:
- **Single eye rod (1)**: One end has a circular eye (ring) with a hole through which the pin passes.
- **Double eye (fork) rod (2)**: One end has two prongs forming a fork that fits around the single eye.
- **Knuckle pin (3)**: A cylindrical pin passing through the aligned holes of the single eye and fork prongs.
- **Cotter pin (4)**: A small tapered pin inserted through a slot at the protruding end of the knuckle pin to prevent its lateral movement.

**Standard proportional relations:**
- Pin diameter: $d_p = 0.5 \times d$
- Eye outer diameter: $d_e = 0.75 \times d$
- Eye thickness: $t = 0.75 \times d$
- Prong width: $b = 1.5 \times d_p$

**Materials:** Mild steel (for rods and pin), case-hardened steel (for pin surface), stainless steel or brass (for cotter pin). [7 marks distributed: 3 for sketch + 2 for proportions + 2 for materials]

---

**(b) Numerical Design — [7 marks]**

**Given:** W = 25 kN = 25,000 N, d = 35 mm, τ_allow = 60 MPa, σ_t_allow = 80 MPa

**Step 1: Calculate pin diameter using empirical rule**

$$
d_p = 0.5 \times d = 0.5 \times 35 = 17.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Standardize to **d_p = 18 mm** (next standard size).

**Step 2: Check pin in double shear**

The knuckle pin is in **double shear** (sheared at two cross-sections by the two prongs).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{pin} &= 2 \times \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 18^2}{2} = 508.94 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\tau_{induced} = \frac{W}{A_{pin}} = \frac{25{,}000}{508.94} = 49.12 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since 49.12 MPa < 60 MPa (allowable), the pin is **SAFE in shear**. ✓

**Step 3: Calculate eye outer diameter**

$$
d_e = 0.75 \times d = 0.75 \times 35 = 26.25 \text{ mm} \approx 27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Check tensile stress in eye**

The eye is pulled in tension across its weakest section, which is the tangential section near the hole.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Width of resisting section} &= d_e - d_p = 27 - 18 = 9 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t = 0.75 \times d = 0.75 \times 35 = 26.25 \text{ mm} \approx 26 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eye} &= (d_e - d_p) \times t = 9 \times 26 = 234 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{W}{A_{eye}} = \frac{25{,}000}{234} = 106.84 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since **106.84 MPa > 80 MPa** (allowable), the eye **FAILS in tension**. ✗

**Step 5: Redesign — increase eye thickness to 32 mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eye} &= (d_e - d_p) \times t = 9 \times 32 = 288 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{25{,}000}{288} = 86.81 \text{ MPa}
$$

Still marginally high. Increase $t$ to **35 mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eye} &= 9 \times 35 = 315 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{25{,}000}{315} = 79.37 \text{ MPa} \leq 80 \text{ MPa} \checkmark
$$

**Final Design:** $d_p = 18$ mm, $d_e = 27$ mm, $t = 35$ mm, $b = 1.5 \times 18 = 27$ mm. [7 marks: 2 for pin, 2 for first design check, 2 for redesign, 1 for final dimensions]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure to model a **Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling** in any 3D CAD software. List all components and their dimensional proportions for a shaft of diameter **d = 45 mm**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the constructional features of a **Plummer Block** with a neat sketch. State its standard proportions and list any **three industrial applications**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Modelling Procedure for Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling — [7 marks]**

**Components list (1 mark):**
1. Two flange hubs (cast iron)
2. Two shafts (driver and driven)
3. Four to six mild steel pins
4. Four to six rubber/leather bushes
5. Two parallel keys

**Dimensional proportions for d = 45 mm (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= 2 \times 45 = 90 \text{ mm (outer flange diameter)} \\
t_f &= 0.5 \times 45 = 22.5 \approx 23 \text{ mm (flange thickness)} \\
D_{hub} &= 1.5 \times 45 = 67.5 \approx 68 \text{ mm (hub diameter)} \\
D_p &= 1.5 \times D_o = 135 \text{ mm (pin pitch circle)} \\
n &= 6 \text{ pins} \\
d_{pin} &= 0.25 \times d = 11.25 \approx 12 \text{ mm (pin diameter)} \\
L_{bush} &= 1.5 \times d_{pin} = 18 \text{ mm (bush length)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step modelling procedure (4 marks):**

1. Open SolidWorks → New Part → sketch flange cross-section: outer circle Ø90 mm, hub circle Ø45 mm, six equispaced Ø13 mm clearance holes on Ø135 PCD, keyway 12×4 mm. Extrude by 23 mm. Add counterbore for pin heads. **Save as `Flange_Flexible_Coupling_A`.** (1 mark)

2. Mirror the flange (using **Mirror Part** in part file) to create the second identical flange. **Save as `Flange_Flexible_Coupling_B`.** (0.5 mark)

3. Model the **pin** as a cylinder Ø12 × 35 mm (length includes head, shank, and nut thread portion). **Save as `Coupling_Pin`.** (0.5 mark)

4. Model the **bush** as a hollow cylinder: outer Ø13 mm, inner Ø12 mm, length 18 mm. Use a flexible-looking colour (grey/black). **Save as `Coupling_Bush`.** (0.5 mark)

5. Create a new Assembly. Insert Flange A, fix at origin. Insert Shaft A into Flange A hub with concentric + coincident. Insert pin into one clearance hole. (0.5 mark)

6. Insert bush over pin (concentric). Insert Flange B over bush (concentric + face coincident). Insert remaining 5 pins and 5 bushes in the same pattern. (1 mark)

7. Validate by **rotating Flange B** about the shaft axis — the bushes should flex visibly (rubber colour). Create exploded view along the shaft axis. Generate drawing with BOM. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Plummer Block — [7 marks]**

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A plummer block consists of:
- A **cast iron base (pedestal)** with a semicircular saddle for shaft seating, two mounting holes at the bottom for floor bolting, and two vertical cap-bolt holes
- A **top cap** that completes the bearing housing and is bolted down
- Two **white-metal or bronze bearing bushes** lining the saddle
- **Cap bolts** (typically 2) holding the cap to the base
- **Foundation bolts** (typically 2) holding the base to the floor/machine frame

**Standard proportions (3 marks) — for shaft diameter d:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{base} &= (4 \text{ to } 5) \times d \\
W_{base} &= (2.5 \text{ to } 3) \times d \\
H_{base} &= 1.5 \times d \quad \text{(up to shaft centre line)} \\
H_{total} &= 2 \times d \quad \text{(including cap)} \\
\text{Cap bolt C-C distance} &= 2 \times d \\
\text{Floor bolt C-C distance} &= 2 \times d \quad \text{(or } 0.75 \times L_{base}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Three industrial applications (2 marks):**
1. **Conveyor systems** in mining and bulk material handling — supports long line shafts at intermediate points
2. **Rolling mills** in steel plants — supports work rolls and back-up rolls
3. **Paper machine dryers** — supports the large drying cylinders and felt rolls
4. (Bonus: Pump shafts, fan shafts, gearbox input/output shafts)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 
> **Mistake 1:** Writing "D = 2d" for flange outer diameter as "D = 2D" by typo — this is read as D = 2D → D = 0, which is impossible. Always write the equation and then substitute values separately.
> 
> **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert kN to N in the stress formula. **25 kN ÷ mm² ≠ 25 N/mm²**. You must write **25,000 N ÷ 234 mm² = 106.84 N/mm² = 106.84 MPa**. This single unit error costs 2 marks.
> 
> **Mistake 3:** In a knuckle joint, students often use the **single-shear** area for the pin instead of **double-shear** area. The fork provides TWO shear planes — divide by 2π in the area formula, not π. Marks lost: 2.
> 
> **Mistake 4:** Failing to **standardize** calculated dimensions. $d_p = 17.5$ mm must be rounded to 18 mm in a real design; $t = 26.25$ mm must be rounded to 27 mm. Drawing dimensions without rounding loses **consistency marks**.
> 
> **Mistake 5:** In the CAD lab, forgetting to **fix the first part** in the assembly. Without fixing, every part moves and the assembly never settles. The examiner specifically tests this — you lose 3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- [ ] **Six assemblies covered:** Socket-Spigot (pipe), Knuckle (linkage), Rigid Flange (coupling), Bushed Pin Flexible (coupling), Plummer Block (bearing), Screw Jack (lifting)
- [ ] **Universal rule for shaft-based assemblies:** Outer diameter = 2 × shaft diameter (D = 2d)
- [ ] **Universal rule for hubs:** Hub length = 1.5 × shaft diameter
- [ ] **Knuckle joint:** Pin in **double shear** — always divide pin area by 2, not by 4
- [ ] **Knuckle joint proportions:** d_p = 0.5d, d_e = 0.75d, t = 0.75d, b = 1.5d_p
- [ ] **Flange coupling bolts:** 4 bolts for d ≤ 40 mm, 6 bolts for d > 40 mm
- [ ] **Flange coupling thickness:** t_f = 0.5d
- [ ] **Flexible coupling advantage:** Tolerates angular misalignment up to 1° and parallel misalignment up to 0.2 mm
- [ ] **Plummer block base:** 4d to 5d long, 2.5d to 3d wide, 1.5d tall
- [ ] **Plummer block cap bolt distance:** 2d centre-to-centre
- [ ] **Screw jack lead:** 10 mm to 20 mm typical — one revolution = one lead of vertical movement
- [ ] **DOF concept:** 6 DOFs for free body, 4 DOFs for cylindrical symmetry parts
- [ ] **Bottom-up vs Top-down:** Use bottom-up for standard parts (bolts, nuts, bearings)
- [ ] **Constraint rules:** Concentric for radial alignment, Coincident for axial alignment, use both together for cylindrical fits
- [ ] **Validation step:** Always drag-test the assembly to ensure only intended DOFs remain
- [ ] **Exploded view:** Direction must be along the functional separation axis (shaft axis for couplings, pin axis for knuckles, vertical for plummer/screw jack)
- [ ] **Drawing deliverables:** Orthographic views + Sectional view + BOM table + Balloon callouts (all four required for full marks)
- [ ] **KTU software:** SolidWorks / CATIA / NX / Fusion 360 — choose one and master it
- [ ] **Units:** Always mm throughout; convert kN to N before dividing by mm²

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Modeling** is a 3D geometric representation technique used in Computer-Aided Design (CAD) where an object is defined by its bounding surfaces (faces), edges, and vertices, rather than by a fully enclosed solid volume. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PCMEL307 – Computer Aided Machine Drawing & Modelling*, surface modeling is the intermediate stage between **wireframe modeling** (which only contains edges/curves) and **solid modeling** (which contains complete volumetric information such as mass, density, and inertia).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**  
> *"Surface modelling is a technique where the skin/boundary of a 3D part is generated using analytical surfaces (planes, cylinders, cones, spheres, tori) and free-form surfaces (Bézier, B-Spline, NURBS), enabling the creation of complex aesthetic and aerodynamic shapes."*

A surface is mathematically represented as a parametric two-dimensional entity:

$$
S(u,v) = \big[\,X(u,v),\; Y(u,v),\; Z(u,v)\,\big]
$$

where $u \in [u_{\min}, u_{\max}]$ and $v \in [v_{\min}, v_{\max}]$ are the two independent parameters, and $X, Y, Z$ are the coordinate functions that map the 2D parameter space into 3D Cartesian space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Labs:** A surface model can be **open** (e.g., a sheet-metal fender) or **closed** (e.g., a helmet shell). It can be converted into a solid later using the *Close Surface* or *Thicken* commands.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon vs. Clay" Intuition

Imagine a **soap bubble** floating in the air. It has a *skin* (the surface) but no definite thickness or mass. You can describe its shape perfectly using only the skin — the curvature, the highlights, the way light reflects off it. That is **surface modeling**.

- A **wireframe** is like a *wire sculpture* — only the skeleton.
- A **surface model** is the **stretched cloth** over the wire — a thin skin with shape but no thickness.
- A **solid model** is the **clay sculpture** with mass — fully filled, with computable volume, mass, and inertia.

A real-world example: when automotive designers at a company like **Tata Motors** or **Toyota** first digitally sculpt a fender, they do so using **surfaces**, not solids, because:
1. Surfaces are mathematically lighter and smoother.
2. Aesthetic curvature (G2/G3 continuity) is easier to control.
3. Class-A surfacing workflows (used in the automotive industry) depend on NURBS surfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Surface modeling = drawing the *outer skin* of a 3D object so precisely that you can almost see the reflection of light gliding across it. It is the language spoken in automotive, aerospace, consumer-product, and industrial-design industries.

## 1.3 Key Terminology to Memorize (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Face** | A single bounded surface patch | ★★★ |
| **Edge** | A curve where two faces meet | ★★★ |
| **Vertex** | A point where two or more edges meet | ★★ |
| **Trimmed Surface** | A surface with part of its parameter space removed | ★★★★ |
| **NURBS** | Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline — industry-standard surface math | ★★★★★ |
| **Continuity (G0–G3, C0–C2)** | How smoothly two surfaces join | ★★★★★ |
| **Loft** | A surface connecting two or more profile curves | ★★★★★ |
| **Sweep** | A surface generated by moving a profile along a path | ★★★★ |
| **Blend/Fillet** | A smooth transition surface between two existing faces | ★★★★★ |
| **Generative Surface Design (GSD)** | The CATIA workbench used in KTU labs | ★★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold:**  
> - **Class-A surface tolerance: ≤ 0.01 mm** (automotive exterior panels).  
> - **Standard NURBS degree: 3 (cubic)** for smooth free-form surfaces.  
> - **Default parameter range: $u, v \in [0, 1]$** in most CAD systems.  
> - **G2 (curvature) continuity is the *minimum* standard** for visible Class-A surfaces in production vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How a CAD Surface is Built

A modern CAD surface is fundamentally a **B-Spline (or NURBS) patch**. The creation pipeline is:

1. **Profile Sketches** — 2D curves drawn in the *Sketcher* workbench.
2. **Guide Curves** — additional 2D/3D curves that govern the shape of the swept/lofted surface.
3. **Surface Generation Command** — *Loft*, *Sweep*, *Revolve*, *Fill*, *Blend*, *Multi-Section Surface*, etc.
4. **Surface Editing** — *Trim*, *Split*, *Extend*, *Match* (continuity control).
5. **Join / Heal** — merging multiple surface patches into a single closed shell.
6. **Thicken / Close Surface** — converting the open surface skin into a solid for manufacturing.

### 2.1.1 The B-Spline Surface Equation (High-Yield)

A B-Spline surface is a piecewise polynomial defined on a parametric grid. For a bi-cubic surface of degree $p = q = 3$:

$$
S(u,v) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u) \, N_{j,q}(v) \, P_{i,j}
$$

where:
- $P_{i,j}$ are the **control points** (the "control polygon" you see in CATIA).
- $N_{i,p}(u)$ and $N_{j,q}(v)$ are the **B-Spline basis functions** of degree $p$ and $q$.
- $u$ and $v$ are the parametric coordinates.

The basis function is defined by the **Cox–de Boor recursion**:

$$
N_{i,0}(u) =
\begin{cases}
1, & u_i \le u < u_{i+1} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i} \, N_{i,p-1}(u) \;+\; \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}} \, N_{i+1,p-1}(u)
$$

where $u_i$ are the entries of the **knot vector** $U = \{u_0, u_1, \ldots, u_{n+p+1}\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **NURBS** (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline) generalises the above by attaching a **weight** $w_{i,j}$ to each control point:

$$
S(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}\,P_{i,j}}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{m} N_{i,p}(u)\,N_{j,q}(v)\,w_{i,j}}
$$

> This rational form is what allows NURBS to represent **conic sections exactly** (a circle, an ellipse, a parabola) — which pure B-Splines cannot.

## 2.2 Surface Continuity — The Heart of Class-A Modeling

Continuity defines *how smoothly* two adjacent surfaces meet. It is the single most tested theoretical concept in KTU viva and written exams.

| Continuity | Geometric Meaning | Tangent Match? | Curvature Match? | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **G0 / C0** | Position continuity — surfaces meet at a point/edge | No | No | Rough castings, hidden internals |
| **G1 / C1** | Tangent continuity — surfaces share a common tangent direction | Yes | No | Most engineering parts |
| **G2 / C2** | Curvature continuity — surfaces share the same radius of curvature at the join | Yes | Yes | **Class-A automotive body panels** |
| **G3 / C3** | Rate of change of curvature matches | Yes | Yes (and higher) | Premium aesthetic surfaces, concept cars |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Geometric continuity (G)** with **Parametric continuity (C)**.  
> - **C1** = the *parametric* derivatives match → automatic G1.  
> - **G1** = only the *tangent direction* matches; magnitudes of derivatives can differ.  
> Always write the **stronger** version if the question does not specify.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Meaning | Use in KTU Lab |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S(u,v) = [X(u,v), Y(u,v), Z(u,v)]$ | Parametric surface definition | Theory questions |
| 2 | $\sum N_{i,p}(u) = 1$ for all $u$ | Partition of unity (B-Spline) | Justification of surface validity |
| 3 | $S_{\text{NURBS}} = \dfrac{\sum w_{ij} N_i N_j P_{ij}}{\sum w_{ij} N_i N_j}$ | Rational form for conics | Helmet, mouse, fender |
| 4 | $u_i \le u \le u_{n+p+1}$ | Knot vector span | Surface definition |
| 5 | $\Delta G = G_{\text{after}} - G_{\text{before}}$ | Continuity grade notation | Match / Blend commands |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{(1 + (y')^2)^{3/2}}{\vert y'' \vert}$ | Radius of curvature (2D) | Justifying G2 quality |
| 7 | $V = \oint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$ | Surface integral — used in solid conversion | Theory |
| 8 | $\mathbf{n} = \dfrac{\partial S / \partial u \times \partial S / \partial v}{\Vert \partial S / \partial u \times \partial S / \partial v \Vert}$ | Unit surface normal | Shell/Thicken |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions in Surface Modeling:**  
> - Closed surface → all edges must be *healed* (no gaps ≥ 0.001 mm).  
> - Periodic surface → the first and last control points must coincide.  
> - G2 surface → curvature plot must show no abrupt jumps at the join.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Surface-Modeled Component | Why Surface Modeling? |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata, Hyundai)** | Fender, bonnet, door, bumper | Class-A aesthetics, aerodynamic drag reduction (target $C_d \le 0.30$) |
| **Aerospace (HAL, Boeing, Airbus)** | Fuselage skin, wing, radome | Weight minimisation, laminar flow |
| **Consumer Electronics (Apple, Samsung)** | Mouse, phone back, earbuds | Ergonomic, premium feel |
| **Protective Equipment (Studds, Steelbird, ISI Helmets)** | Motorcycle helmet | Impact energy distribution, aerodynamics |
| **Medical Devices** | Prosthetic socket, hearing aid shell | Anatomical conformity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure for Surface Modeling — Helmet, Mouse, Automobile Fender

## 3.1 Universal Workflow for Surface Modeling in CATIA / SolidWorks / NX

The following **6-step pipeline** is the standard industry (and KTU lab) workflow. Memorise it — it is the answer to nearly every "explain the procedure" question.

**Step 1 — Reference Geometry Setup**  
Create the three default orthogonal planes (XY, YZ, ZX) and any auxiliary construction planes/sketch planes required.

**Step 2 — 2D Profile / Wireframe Construction**  
In the *Sketcher* (or *Wireframe & Surface Design* workbench in CATIA), draw the master profile(s) and guide curves. Use splines, conics, and lines.

**Step 3 — Surface Generation**  
Apply the appropriate command:
- **Revolve** → axisymmetric shells (helmet body, simple dome).
- **Sweep** → translating a profile along a path (mouse side curve).
- **Loft** → blending between two or more cross-sections (fender).
- **Fill / Multi-Section Surface** → closing gaps.

**Step 4 — Surface Editing (Trim, Split, Extend, Match)**  
Refine the surface — cut openings for vents, visor, wheel arch.

**Step 5 — Continuity / Quality Control**  
Use *Curvature Analysis* (the iconic "rainbow" or "zebra" plot) to verify G2 continuity. Use the *Match Surface* tool to enforce G1/G2 between adjacent faces.

**Step 6 — Join, Close, Thicken (Solid Conversion)**  
Use *Join* (CATIA) or *Knit Surface* (SolidWorks) → *Close Surface* → *Thicken* to obtain a manufacturable solid of the desired shell thickness (typically **1.5 mm – 3.0 mm** for helmets/fenders).

---

## 3.2 Detailed Procedure — Motorcycle Helmet (Full-Face)

A full-face helmet is essentially a **revolved + lofted + trimmed surface** with vent openings.

### Stage A — Master Profile

1. Activate the **Front Plane** (XY-plane).
2. Sketch the side-view silhouette of the helmet: a smooth closed curve from the chin guard, up over the crown, down to the rear neck.
3. Use a **cubic spline** (degree 3) with **6–8 control points** to capture the aerodynamic teardrop shape.
4. Add construction points to mark the visor opening, chin vent, and top vent.

### Stage B — Generate the Outer Shell

1. Use **Revolve** (axis = vertical Y-axis through the helmet centre) on the closed silhouette to create the basic dome.
   - Result: a single revolution surface.

2. Alternatively, use **Loft** between three cross-sections (front, middle, rear) for a more sculpted shape — this is the **Class-A method** preferred in industry.

### Stage C — Generate the Inner Shell

1. Offset the outer shell inward by **15 mm – 20 mm** (the EPS liner gap).
2. Use the *Offset Surface* command.

### Stage D — Cut the Visor and Vent Openings

1. Sketch a curved cut-out for the visor on the front plane.
2. Use **Split Surface** with the sketch as the cutting element.
3. Repeat for the chin vent (rectangular) and top vent (small oval).

### Stage E — Edge Blend and Match

1. Apply **Blend** (or *Fillet Surface* in SolidWorks) with a radius of **3 mm – 5 mm** on all hard edges to give a soft, continuous G2 look.
2. Run **Curvature Analysis** — the rainbow bands should flow smoothly.

### Stage F — Solid Conversion

1. **Join** the outer and inner surfaces (the inner one becomes a separate body).
2. **Thicken** the outer shell outward by **3.0 mm** (typical shell thickness for ABS/PC material).
3. Save as `.CATPart` and export to `.STEP` for the next lab exercise (FEA analysis of impact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Numbers to Memorise (Helmet):** Shell thickness ≈ **3 mm**, EPS liner ≈ **15–20 mm**, visor opening radius ≈ **60 mm**, chin vent ≈ **80 × 25 mm**, top vent ≈ **40 × 12 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Procedure — Computer Mouse

A computer mouse is a classic **multi-section lofted surface** with a centre-button split.

### Stage A — Three Section Curves

1. On the **Top Plane (XZ)**, sketch three profiles at three stations:
   - **Front profile** (small, rounded — for the palm rest).
   - **Middle profile** (widest — for the button area).
   - **Rear profile** (slightly narrower, sloping down).
2. The profiles should be **non-planar** and tilted upward toward the rear (ergonomic palm grip).

### Stage B — Guide Curves

1. Sketch two **side-view guide curves** on the YZ plane to control the upward sweep of the mouse.
2. These guides ensure the loft does not "twist" awkwardly.

### Stage C — Loft the Upper Shell

1. Activate the *Generative Surface Design* workbench in CATIA (or *Surfaces → Lofted Surface* in SolidWorks).
2. Select the three profiles in order.
3. Select the two guide curves.
4. Click **OK** — a smooth, ergonomic upper shell appears.

### Stage D — Mirror to Create the Lower Shell

1. Mirror the upper shell across the top plane (XY).
2. Apply a small **fillet of 8 mm** at the parting line (where the upper and lower halves meet in real life) to soften the join.

### Stage E — Trim the Scroll-Wheel Opening

1. Sketch a small oval cut-out (~12 mm × 6 mm) on the central plane.
2. Use **Split Surface** to remove the upper half of the oval.
3. Add a separate **scroll-wheel cylinder** (Ø 8 mm, length 18 mm) as a placeholder.

### Stage F — Continuity Check & Solidify

1. Apply **Match** with **G2** between upper and lower shells.
2. **Thicken** the upper shell by **1.5 mm** and the lower shell by **1.5 mm**.
3. The cavity between them automatically becomes the **interior** where the PCB, switch, and battery will sit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix — Why Loft, not Revolve, for a Mouse:**  
> - **Revolve** → axisymmetric → would make the mouse look like a spinning top.  
> - **Loft** between non-coaxial, non-planar profiles → produces the ergonomic asymmetric dome a real mouse has.  
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU viva question**.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Procedure — Automobile Fender

An automobile fender is the most complex of the three because it must blend smoothly with the bonnet, door, A-pillar, and bumper, and it must arch over the wheel.

### Stage A — Reference Geometry and Datum Planes

1. Create the **vehicle coordinate system**: X = longitudinal (front–rear), Y = lateral (left–right), Z = vertical (up).
2. Create the **wheel centre datum**, the **ground plane (XY)**, and the **wheel-arch plane** (vertical, passing through the wheel centre).

### Stage B — Build the Five Master Curves

Draw five large, sweeping 3D curves using the *Wireframe* workbench:

1. **Upper Character Line** — the sharp crease running from the headlamp to the door handle area.
2. **Wheel Arch Curve** — a half-ellipse (semi-major $a \approx 380$ mm, semi-minor $b \approx 200$ mm for a typical sedan).
3. **Bonnet-to-Fender Seam** — a gentle S-curve.
4. **Lower Side Sill Line** — connects the fender bottom to the side sill.
5. **A-Pillar / Wing Edge Curve** — connects the fender to the A-pillar.

### Stage C — Generate the Main Fender Skin

1. Use **Multi-Section Surface** (CATIA) or **Lofted-Boss** (SolidWorks) with the five curves as sections.
2. Make sure each section curve has **at least 20 control points** to ensure the surface is smooth, not faceted.

### Stage D — Sculpt the Wheel-Arch Opening

1. Sketch a wheel-arch profile (semi-ellipse with $a = 380$ mm, $b = 200$ mm) on the wheel-arch plane.
2. Use **Split Surface** with this profile as the cutter.
3. The outer half is the fender skin; the inner half is discarded (or used as a wheel-house liner).

### Stage E — Edge Blend and Class-A Finishing

1. Apply a **Variable-Radius Fillet Surface** along the upper character line: radius varies from **5 mm** (near headlamp) to **12 mm** (near the door). This is what gives the car its "muscular shoulder."
2. Apply a **constant 3 mm blend** at the wheel arch to soften the cut.
3. Use **Match** with **G2 continuity** at the bonnet-fender seam.

### Stage F — Thickness Conversion

1. Use **Thicken Surface** to give the fender a **0.65 mm – 0.8 mm** sheet-metal thickness (typical for a stamped steel fender).
2. For an aluminium/composite fender, use **1.2 mm – 1.5 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Numbers (Fender):** Wheel arch semi-major ≈ **380 mm**, semi-minor ≈ **200 mm**, sheet thickness ≈ **0.7 mm** (steel) / **1.2 mm** (aluminium), upper character line radius ≈ **5–12 mm (variable)**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Parametric Equation of a Helmet Silhouette (Bezier Form)

The side profile of a helmet can be expressed as a **cubic Bézier curve** with four control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$:

$$
B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t \, P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \, P_2 + t^3 P_3, \quad t \in [0,1]
$$

Let us place the control points (in mm, with origin at the helmet's lowest chin point):
- $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$ — chin tip.
- $P_1 = (-20, 80, 0)$ — chin-guard pull-back.
- $P_2 = (-60, 230, 0)$ — crown pull-back.
- $P_3 = (0, 260, 0)$ — top-rear point (back of head).

For any $t \in [0,1]$, the Bézier point is:

$$
B_x(t) = (1-t)^3 (0) + 3(1-t)^2 t (-20) + 3(1-t) t^2 (-60) + t^3 (0)
$$

$$
B_x(t) = -60 \, t (1-t)^2 - 180 \, t^2 (1-t)
$$

$$
B_x(t) = -60 \, t (1-t)\big[(1-t) + 3t\big] = -60 \, t (1-t)(1 + 2t)
$$

$$
B_y(t) = (1-t)^3 (0) + 3(1-t)^2 t (80) + 3(1-t) t^2 (230) + t^3 (260)
$$

$$
B_y(t) = 240 \, t (1-t)^2 + 690 \, t^2 (1-t) + 260 \, t^3
$$

Evaluating at a few sample values to verify the curve:

| $t$ | $B_x(t)$ (mm) | $B_y(t)$ (mm) | Physical location |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 0 | 0 | Chin tip |
| 0.25 | −10.3 | 85.0 | Mid chin guard |
| 0.50 | −22.5 | 158.8 | Cheek area |
| 0.75 | −21.1 | 218.1 | Upper crown |
| 1.00 | 0 | 260.0 | Rear crown |

The negative $B_x$ values indicate the silhouette bulges *forward* (toward the visor) in the cheek area, which is the classic helmet ergonomic bulge. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Examiners love to give a 7-mark sub-question asking *"Derive the parametric equation of the profile used for surface modeling"* — and a Bézier-style derivation scoring 6–7 marks is the cleanest way to answer it.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Which Surface Command for Which Geometry?

| Component | Dominant Surface Command | Reason | Typical # of Patches |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Helmet** | Revolve + Loft (hybrid) | Mostly axisymmetric, with vent cuts | 1 (shell) + 1 (liner) + 3 (vents) |
| **Mouse** | Multi-Section Loft | Asymmetric, ergonomic dome | 1 (upper) + 1 (lower) |
| **Fender** | Multi-Section Surface + Blend | Free-form, must blend with 4 adjacent panels | 1 main + 1 wheel arch + 2 blends |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Surface Modeling**. How is it different from **Wireframe Modeling** and **Solid Modeling**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Surface Modeling** is a 3D CAD representation technique in which an object is described by its outer *skin* — i.e., the faces, edges, and vertices that bound its geometry — without necessarily defining its interior volume.

| Aspect | Wireframe | Surface | Solid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data stored | Edges only | Faces + edges | Faces + edges + volume |
| Mass / Volume | ✗ Not computable | ✗ Not directly (only after thicken) | ✓ Computable |
| Visualization | Ambiguous (hidden lines removed) | Unambiguous shaded view | Unambiguous shaded view |
| Industry use | Skeletal layouts, schematics | Aesthetic & aerodynamic shapes | Functional engineering parts |

*(1 mark for the surface-modeling definition, 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for the conclusion.)* ✓

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the difference between **Geometric Continuity (G1, G2)** and **Parametric Continuity (C1, C2)**. Which one is preferred for Class-A automotive surfaces and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Parametric continuity (C1, C2)** requires the *derivatives* of the surface with respect to the parameter $u$ (or $v$) to match in both *direction* and *magnitude*. C1 → first derivatives match, C2 → second derivatives match.
- **Geometric continuity (G1, G2)** only requires the *tangent direction* (G1) or the *curvature* (G2) to match — the magnitudes can differ.

For Class-A automotive surfaces, **G2 (curvature continuity) is the *minimum* accepted standard**, because:
1. It produces a smooth, kink-free reflection under showroom lighting.
2. It is achievable with simpler (and faster) surface patches than strict C2.
3. It is the automotive industry convention (SAE J1828).

*(1 mark for the definitions, 1 mark for the comparison, 1 mark for the Class-A justification.)* ✓

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **step-by-step procedure to surface-model a full-face motorcycle helmet** using *Generative Surface Design* in CATIA V5. Mention the surface commands used, the typical dimensions, and explain how G2 continuity is achieved at the vent openings.

**OR**

**Question B (14 Marks)** — see below.

#### Part (a) — Procedure & Surface Commands (7 Marks)

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Create three orthogonal datum planes (XY, YZ, ZX). Activate *Generative Surface Design* workbench.
2. **Master profile (1 mark):** Sketch the side-view silhouette of the helmet (closed cubic spline) on the YZ plane. Use **6–8 control points**.
3. **Outer shell (1 mark):** Apply **Revolve Surface** with the vertical Y-axis as the axis. The basic dome is obtained.
4. **Inner liner (1 mark):** Apply **Offset Surface** at a distance of **18 mm** inward to get the EPS-liner surface.
5. **Vent cuts (1 mark):** Sketch the visor opening (radius ≈ 60 mm), chin vent (80 × 25 mm), and top vent (40 × 12 mm). Use **Split Surface** with each sketch as the cutting element.
6. **Edge blend (1 mark):** Apply **Fillet Surface** of radius **4 mm** to soften the cut edges.
7. **Solid conversion (1 mark):** Use **Join**, then **Thicken** the outer shell by **3 mm**, the inner shell by **5 mm**, to obtain the final part.

#### Part (b) — G2 Continuity at Vent Openings (7 Marks)

1. **Identify the join (1 mark):** The vent opening is the curve where the **trimmed outer shell** meets the **filleted vent edge**.
2. **Set the continuity target (1 mark):** Activate the *Match Surface* command. In the *Type* pull-down, select **Curvature (G2)**.
3. **Pick the two surfaces (1 mark):** Select the main shell and the fillet surface at the vent.
4. **Tension slider (1 mark):** Move the tension slider to **1.0** to enforce full G2 curvature matching.
5. **Verify with curvature analysis (1 mark):** Switch the view to *Curvature Analysis* with the comb/rainbow mode. The colour bands must flow continuously across the join — no abrupt jumps in colour gradient.
6. **Reflectance check (1 mark):** Use the *Reflectance Lines* command (CATIA). The white reflection lines must cross the join in a smooth, unbroken curve. If they kink, return to Step 2 and increase the number of control points on the trim curve.
7. **Tolerance (1 mark):** Ensure the maximum deviation between the original and matched surfaces is **≤ 0.01 mm** (Class-A standard).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the workbench name** (e.g., "Generative Surface Design"). Students who say "use CATIA" without naming the workbench lose 1 mark.  
> 2. **Confusing G2 with C2.** G2 is the *industry* standard; C2 is *over-constrained*.  
> 3. **Skipping the verification step.** Just saying "apply Match" is not enough — examiners want *Curvature Analysis* and *Reflectance Lines* mentioned.  
> 4. **Missing dimensions.** Always quote numerical values (shell thickness 3 mm, liner 18 mm, etc.) — even if approximate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the **surface modeling of a computer mouse** using the *Multi-Section Surface* (Loft) command. Include the role of guide curves, the steps to model the scroll-wheel opening, and the use of *Match Surface* to obtain G2 continuity between the upper and lower shells. Provide a neat sketch of the profile curves.

#### Part (a) — Profiles, Guides, and Loft (7 Marks)

1. **Workbench (1 mark):** Open *Part Design* → *Sketcher* for profiles, then switch to *Generative Surface Design* for the loft.
2. **Three section profiles (1 mark each × 3):**
   - Front profile: small, rounded, 30 mm wide × 18 mm tall.
   - Middle profile: widest at 65 mm × 35 mm.
   - Rear profile: 55 mm × 30 mm.
   - Each is a closed cubic spline.
3. **Two guide curves (1 mark):** Sketched on the YZ plane, controlling the upward palm-rest sweep.
4. **Multi-section loft (1 mark):** Select the three profiles in order, then the two guides, click *OK*. The upper shell is created.
5. **Mirror (1 mark):** Mirror the upper shell across the top plane (XY) to get the lower shell. The parting line is on the symmetry plane.

#### Part (b) — Scroll-Wheel Opening & G2 Match (7 Marks)

1. **Sketch the scroll-wheel cut-out (1 mark):** On the central parting plane, sketch an oval of **12 mm × 6 mm**, centred **30 mm from the front** of the mouse.
2. **Split the upper shell (1 mark):** Use *Split Surface* with the oval as the cutter. Discard the upper half of the oval.
3. **Add the scroll-wheel placeholder (1 mark):** Create a cylinder Ø **8 mm**, length **18 mm**, placed inside the oval.
4. **Match between upper and lower (1 mark):** Activate *Match Surface*. Select the bottom face of the upper shell and the top face of the lower shell along the parting curve.
5. **Set G2 (1 mark):** Pull-down → *Curvature*. Tension = 1.0.
6. **Verify (1 mark):** Run *Curvature Analysis* and *Reflectance Lines* — the rainbow bands and the white reflection lines must be continuous across the parting line.
7. **Thicken (1 mark):** Apply *Thicken* of **1.5 mm** to both shells. The internal cavity is the housing for the PCB, switch, and battery.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — READ BEFORE THE EXAM**

- ☐ **Surface Modeling** = 3D representation using the *skin* of an object (faces + edges), not the solid volume.
- ☐ The three modelling paradigms, in order of increasing information: **Wireframe → Surface → Solid**.
- ☐ A **NURBS** surface is defined by control points, weights, knot vector, and degree (typically degree 3 = cubic).
- ☐ **Loft** (Multi-Section Surface) is used when the part has *different* cross-sections at different stations (mouse, fender).
- ☐ **Revolve** is used when the part is *axisymmetric* (helmet dome).
- ☐ **Sweep** is used when a profile is *translated* along a path.
- ☐ **G0** = position continuity; **G1** = tangent continuity; **G2** = curvature continuity; **G3** = rate-of-change-of-curvature continuity.
- ☐ **G2 is the *minimum* standard for Class-A automotive surfaces.**
- ☐ **Curvature Analysis** (the rainbow comb) and **Reflectance Lines** (the white bands) are the two verification tools for surface quality.
- ☐ **Match Surface** is the command used to enforce G1/G2/G3 between two adjacent surfaces.
- ☐ The standard **Class-A tolerance** is **≤ 0.01 mm**.
- ☐ Helmet shell thickness ≈ **3 mm**; EPS liner ≈ **15–20 mm**.
- ☐ Mouse shell thickness ≈ **1.5 mm**; scroll wheel Ø ≈ **8 mm**.
- ☐ Fender (steel) thickness ≈ **0.7 mm**; aluminium ≈ **1.2 mm**; wheel arch semi-axes ≈ **380 mm × 200 mm** for a sedan.
- ☐ *Generative Surface Design* (CATIA), *Surfaces* tab (SolidWorks), and *Freeform Modeling* (NX) are the three most common KTU-lab workbenches.
- ☐ Surface modeling is the **only feasible method** for aesthetically complex, free-form, aerodynamic shapes used in automotive, aerospace, and consumer-product industries.
- ☐ A surface model is converted to a solid using **Thicken** (single open skin) or **Close Surface + Thicken** (a fully closed skin).
- ☐ **Continuity pitfall:** Always say G2 (geometric), *not* C2 (parametric), for Class-A surfaces in the KTU exam — unless the question explicitly asks about parameter-derivative matching.
- ☐ In viva: be ready to **draw the side profile of a helmet** with control points, and explain *why* you chose cubic Bézier over quadratic.
- ☐ Remember the *formula* for the Bézier curve (Section 3.5) — it is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation in Part B.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "list the surface commands used," always write them in *chronological order* of the workflow (Reference → Profile → Generate → Edit → Match → Thicken). Examiners reward logical sequence.
