# Parametric Modeling of Standard Parts (Nuts, Bolts, Rivets, Washers)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Parametric Modeling** is a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) methodology in which the geometric features of a 3D model are defined by *parameters* (variables, dimensions, relations, and constraints) rather than by fixed numerical values. Changing the value of a single parameter automatically updates every dependent feature in the model — this is the cornerstone of feature-based, associative solid modeling in NEP 2020 aligned curricula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):** *Parametric modeling of standard parts refers to the generation of 3D solid models of repeatedly used machine elements (hexagonal nuts, hexagonal/square bolts, rivets, washers) using dimensional parameters governed by Indian Standards (IS) and International Organization for Standardization (ISO) recommendations, where a single driving variable — the nominal diameter **D** — controls the entire geometry of the part.*

**Standard Parts** in the context of KTU Machine Drawing are those *off-the-shelf* threaded and unthreaded fasteners whose proportions are governed by standards. The relevant codes most frequently tested in the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMEL307) are:

| Code | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **IS 1364** | Hexagon head bolts, screws and nuts (product grade C) |
| **IS 1363** | Hexagon head bolts, screws and nuts (product grade A & B) |
| **IS 2016** | Plain washers |
| **IS 3063** | Spring lock washers |
| **IS 1929** | Rivet dimensions (snap, pan, countersunk heads) |
| **ISO 4014 / 4017** | Hexagon head bolts and screws |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Master Template" Analogy (Intuitive Explanation):**
> Imagine you bake cookies. A parametric model is the **cookie-cutter template** with one master slider marked "size". If you push the slider from 2 cm to 5 cm, **every** feature — the cookie's outer edge, the inner star pattern, the depth of the embossed text — all change in correct proportion automatically. You never re-draw anything.
>
> In CAD, the **nominal bolt diameter `D`** is the slider. Push `D = 10 mm` to `D = 20 mm` and the across-flats, head height, chamfer, washer inner diameter, and rivet head radius all jump to their correct ISO proportions *without* the designer typing a single extra number. This is the power of parametric modeling.

## 1.3 Why Parametric Modeling is the KTU 2024 Focus

- **Productivity** — one design table drives 50 sizes of the same part family.
- **Standardisation** — dimensions are linked to **IS / ISO** tables, eliminating human error.
- **Design Intent Capture** — the *relations* (e.g., "head height = 0.7 × D") are stored explicitly, not buried in coordinates.
- **Automation** — enables Design Tables, Configurations, and Equations inside SolidWorks / Solid Edge / Fusion 360.
- **NEP 2020 Outcome Linkage** — maps directly to **CO2**: *“Model standard machine components using parametric CAD tools.”*

The four standard parts required by **KTU Module Experiment 2** are:

1. **Hexagonal Nut** (internal thread, full body)
2. **Hexagonal Bolt Head** (external thread, head portion)
3. **Rivets** (snap head, pan head, countersunk head)
4. **Washers** (plain washer and spring lock washer)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parametric Modeling Workflow (Feature-Based Logic)

The standard part is built layer by layer, where every feature is anchored to the global parameter `D`:

1. **Sketch a 2D profile** on the Front Plane using a regular polygon (hexagon / square).
2. **Apply the Across-Flats relation:** $W = f(D)$ (the *driver* equation).
3. **Extrude** the sketch to a height $H = f(D)$.
4. **Apply chamfers** of size $c = 0.15 D$ at 30° to 45° on the top edges.
5. **Cut a thread-representation hole** of diameter $d_h = 0.85 D$ (tapping drill size).
6. **Add a cosmetic thread** sweep if threaded representation is needed.

The design intent — captured as *Equations* inside the CAD tree — guarantees that changing `D` updates **all** dependent features simultaneously.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **single most tested reference** for Module Experiment 2. All units are in **millimetres (mm)** and all are functions of the nominal diameter **D**.

| Across Corners | $A$ | $A = \sqrt{3}\,W = 1.732\,W$ | Geometry |

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Manufacturability (DFM):** Standardised proportions mean a single wrench size fits $W$ across an entire assembly.
- **Interchangeability:** A bolt of $D = 12$ mm from any ISO-certified vendor mates with any nut of $D = 12$ mm.
- **Digital Twin Compatibility:** Parametric models feed directly into **FEA**, **CAM toolpath generation**, and **PLM databases**.
- **Cost Reduction:** Eliminates the need to draw every part from scratch; one family template + a design table is enough.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Across-Flats from Across-Corners for a Hexagon

A regular hexagon inscribed in a circle of radius $R_c$ (the *across-corners* distance) has its *across-flats* distance given by projecting one side onto the perpendicular axis.

**Step 1.** The central angle subtended by one side is $\dfrac{360°}{6} = 60°$.

**Step 2.** Drop a perpendicular from the centre to one side. The half-side length is $R_c \cdot \sin(30°)$.

**Step 3.** The full across-flats width is twice the half-side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 2 \cdot R_c \cdot \sin(30°) \\
  &= 2 \cdot R_c \cdot 0.5 \\
  &= R_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** But standard practice expresses $W$ in terms of the *nominal diameter* $D$ (the shank). Empirical standardisation gives:

$$
W \;=\; 1.5\,D + 3
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore the across-corners distance is:

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{2\,W}{\sqrt{3}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,(1.5\,D + 3)}{\sqrt{3}} \;\approx\; 1.732\,W
$$

> **Conversion Logic (Examinable):** $A = \sqrt{3}\,W$ when $W$ is the apothem (across flats); the standard across-corners for a regular hexagon is always $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ times the apothem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hexagonal Head Height Ratio $H/D$

The IS 1364 head height is derived from the **stress-area** of a hex socket engagement. Empirical relationship:

$$
H \;=\; 0.7\,D \;\; \text{(for Grade A and B)}
$$

For heavy hex nuts (IS 1364 Part 3), the head is **slightly taller**:

$$
H \;=\; 0.8\,D \;\; \text{(heavy series)}
$$

**Numerical example (typical exam problem):**
For a hexagonal bolt with $D = 12$ mm,

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 1.5 \times 12 + 3 \;=\; 18 + 3 \;=\; \mathbf{21 \; mm} \\
A &= 1.732 \times 21 \;=\; \mathbf{36.37 \; mm} \\
H &= 0.7 \times 12 \;=\; \mathbf{8.4 \; mm} \\
c &= 0.15 \times 12 \;=\; \mathbf{1.8 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the standard *M12* hex bolt geometry every KTU student must be able to reproduce on paper in 30 seconds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plain Washer Proportions

A plain washer has two design intents: (a) distribute the clamping load, and (b) prevent the nut from digging into a soft mating surface. The standard (IS 2016) gives the following empirical proportions.

**Step 1.** Outer diameter — chosen to be twice the bolt size to give sufficient bearing area:

$$
d_2 \;=\; 2\,D + 1.5
$$

**Step 2.** Inner diameter — chosen to clear the bolt shank with $\approx 0.5$ mm radial clearance on each side:

$$
d_1 \;=\; D + 2(0.05\,D + 0.25) \;\approx\; 1.1\,D + 0.5
$$

**Step 3.** Thickness — chosen to provide adequate bearing without adding significant stack-up:

$$
t \;=\; 0.15\,D + 0.5
$$

**Numerical example:**
For $D = 10$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= 2 \times 10 + 1.5 \;=\; \mathbf{21.5 \; mm} \\
d_1 &= 1.1 \times 10 + 0.5 \;=\; \mathbf{11.5 \; mm} \\
t   &= 0.15 \times 10 + 0.5 \;=\; \mathbf{2.0 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Snap-Head Rivet Geometry

A snap-head rivet has a **spherical cap** of radius $R$. The head radius is empirically chosen so that the *circumference* of the spherical surface is $1.5 \times$ the shank circumference — this gives a generous bearing collar.

**Step 1.** Head radius is taken as a fraction of shank diameter:

$$
R \;=\; 0.8\,D
$$

**Step 2.** The spherical cap height $h$ is related to $R$ by the sphere geometry. For a circle of radius $R$ in side view, the cap height is:

$$
h \;=\; R - \sqrt{R^2 - (D/2)^2}
$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $R = 0.8\,D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 0.8\,D - \sqrt{(0.8\,D)^2 - (0.5\,D)^2} \\
  &= 0.8\,D - \sqrt{0.64\,D^2 - 0.25\,D^2} \\
  &= 0.8\,D - \sqrt{0.39\,D^2} \\
  &= 0.8\,D - 0.6245\,D \\
  &\approx 0.175\,D
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this geometric value does **not** match the standard. The standard quotes an *empirical* head height of $h = 0.7\,D$ measured from the **shank surface** to the **head apex**, not the spherical-cap height. This is because the manufactured snap head has a *flat top* in addition to the spherical cap.

**Correction (standard practice):**

$$
h_{\text{standard}} \;=\; 0.7\,D \;\; \text{(total head height including the fillet)}
$$

**Numerical example (D = 16 mm):**

$$
R = 0.8 \times 16 = 12.8 \text{ mm}, \qquad h = 0.7 \times 16 = 11.2 \text{ mm}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define parametric modeling. State any two advantages relevant to standard part design.**

**Model Answer:**
Parametric modeling is a CAD technique in which the geometry of a part is defined by *parameters* (dimensions and relations) rather than fixed numeric values. Changing one parameter automatically updates all dependent features.

**Advantages:**
1. **Design Reuse:** A single template can generate an entire family of parts by changing the driving parameter (e.g., nominal diameter $D$).
2. **Error Reduction:** Standardised relations such as $W = 1.5D + 3$ are stored as equations, eliminating manual calculation mistakes and ensuring ISO compliance.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the Indian Standard codes used for (i) hexagonal nuts, (ii) plain washers, and (iii) rivet proportions.**

**Model Answer:**
| Component | Indian Standard |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Hexagonal nuts | **IS 1364** |
| Plain washers | **IS 2016** |
| Rivet proportions | **IS 1929** |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 Compulsory Pattern]`

**(a) Draw the standard proportions of a hexagonal nut of size M20 using the IS 1364 formulas. Show all key dimensions. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the dimensions of a plain washer and a spring lock washer for the same M20 bolt. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
Given $D = 20$ mm. Apply the IS 1364 formulas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 1.5\,D + 3 = 1.5 \times 20 + 3 = 30 + 3 = \mathbf{33 \; mm} \\
A &= 1.732 \times W = 1.732 \times 33 = \mathbf{57.16 \; mm} \\
H &= 0.8\,D = 0.8 \times 20 = \mathbf{16 \; mm} \quad (\text{nut height}) \\
c &= 0.15\,D = 0.15 \times 20 = \mathbf{3 \; mm} \quad (\text{chamfer}) \\
d_h &= 0.85\,D = 0.85 \times 20 = \mathbf{17 \; mm} \quad (\text{tapping drill})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sketches required:**
- Front view: rectangle of width $W = 33$ mm and height $H = 16$ mm with chamfer $c = 3$ mm at top.
- Top view: regular hexagon with across-flats $33$ mm and across-corners $57.16$ mm.
- Internal thread shown as a circle of $\varnothing\, 17$ mm with a smaller $\varnothing\, 15.93$ mm (minor diameter) for the cosmetic thread representation.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Plain Washer (IS 2016):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= 2\,D + 1.5 = 2 \times 20 + 1.5 = \mathbf{41.5 \; mm} \\
d_1 &= 1.1\,D + 0.5 = 1.1 \times 20 + 0.5 = \mathbf{22.5 \; mm} \\
t   &= 0.15\,D + 0.5 = 0.15 \times 20 + 0.5 = \mathbf{3.5 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Spring Lock Washer (IS 3063):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2 &= 1.5\,D + 1.5 = 1.5 \times 20 + 1.5 = \mathbf{31.5 \; mm} \\
d_1 &= 1.1\,D + 0.5 = \mathbf{22.5 \; mm} \\
t   &= 0.15\,D + 0.5 = \mathbf{3.5 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The spring lock washer additionally has a helical gap of $s \ge 0.25\,D = 5$ mm and a square cross-section of side $t = 3.5$ mm.

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) Using IS 1929, derive the proportions of a snap-head rivet of diameter 16 mm. Sketch the rivet showing the spherical head, shank, and head height. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two mild-steel plates of 10 mm thickness each are to be joined by a lap joint using snap-head rivets arranged in diamond pattern. Design the riveted joint. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
Given $D = 16$ mm. Apply the IS 1929 formulas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.8\,D = 0.8 \times 16 = \mathbf{12.8 \; mm} \\
h &= 0.7\,D = 0.7 \times 16 = \mathbf{11.2 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sketch requirements (full marks):**
- Shown in two views: (i) Front view showing the shank of length $L$, the spherical head of radius $R = 12.8$ mm, and total head height $h = 11.2$ mm. (ii) Side view showing the circular cross-section of the head and shank.
- The shank length $L$ is typically $L = 2t + \text{grip allowance}$ where $t$ is the total plate thickness and grip allowance is $6D/100 = 1.04$ mm per joint.

**Model Solution for (b):**
For two plates of $t = 10$ mm each, total plate thickness $= 20$ mm.
Unwin's formula for rivet diameter:

$$
d = 6 \sqrt{t} = 6 \sqrt{10} \approx 19 \text{ mm}
$$

Choose standard $D = 20$ mm.

Pitch (diamond pattern, IS 800):

$$
p = 2.5\,D = 2.5 \times 20 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

Distance between rows of rivets (diamond pattern):

$$
m = 0.6\,p = 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

Efficiency and strength calculations are then performed using the standard IS 800 procedure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula Misuse (–2 marks):** Students often quote $H = 0.7D$ for both *bolts* and *nuts*. The correct values are $H_{bolt} = 0.7D$ and $H_{nut} = 0.8D$. Losing 2 marks here is extremely common.
> 2. **Across-Corners Confusion (–2 marks):** Confusing $W$ (across flats) with $A$ (across corners) — the *corner* is $\sqrt{3}$ times the *flat*, not equal to it. Always sketch a regular hexagon and label both.
> 3. **Unit Omission (–1 mark):** Forgetting to write "mm" after numeric values in a KTU exam loses a mark even if the calculation is correct.
> 4. **Spring Washer Gap (–1 mark):** Drawing a spring washer as a flat washer without the helical gap and square cross-section — both are mandatory for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Driving Parameter:** Always start with the **nominal diameter `D`** — it controls every other dimension in the standard part.
- **Hex Head/Bolt:** $W = 1.5D + 3$ (across flats), $H = 0.7D$ (head height), $A = 1.732 W$ (across corners).
- **Hex Nut:** $W = 1.5D + 3$, $H = 0.8D$ (slightly taller than the bolt head).
- **Square Head:** $W = 1.5D + 3$, $H = 0.75D$.
- **Plain Washer:** $d_1 = 1.1D + 0.5$, $d_2 = 2D + 1.5$, $t = 0.15D + 0.5$.
- **Spring Lock Washer:** $d_1 = 1.1D + 0.5$, $d_2 = 1.5D + 1.5$, $t = 0.15D + 0.5$, helical gap $s \ge 0.25D$, square cross-section.
- **Snap-Head Rivet:** $R = 0.8D$, $h = 0.7D$, spherical top with fillet.
- **Pan-Head Rivet:** $R = 0.8D$, $h = 0.4D$, truncated.
- **Countersunk Rivet:** $75°$ included angle (occasionally $60°$); head sits flush with the plate.
- **Tapping Drill:** $d_h \approx 0.85D$ — used as the cut-feature diameter in the nut body.
- **Indian Standards:** IS 1364 (hex), IS 2016 (plain washer), IS 3063 (spring washer), IS 1929 (rivets), IS 800 (riveted joint design).
- **Always dimension both views** — Front and Top for nuts/bolts; Front and Side for rivets and washers.
- **Always show the chamfer** $c = 0.15D$ on hex nut/bolt — its omission is a guaranteed mark deduction.
- **NEP 2020 CO Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO2** (Model standard machine components) and **CO3** (Apply IS/ISO standards in CAD).
